# Thread: Bio Hacking-What to ask for
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bio-hacking-what-to-ask-for.7368/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LRichard12 — 2013-06-17T14:30:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bio-hacking-what-to-ask-for.7368/post-88231>

Dear all;

I have posted this as a question for Dr. Kruse, but would also appreciate any advice from others if yo're so inclined;

I know you can't practice med online, but could you help me to talk to my doc. He knows nothing about thyroid, depression, leptin, etc. but is willing to write any prescription or do any blood work I ask for (good doctor ehe).

What tests should I request for my problem.

Any suggested method of treatment I could discuss with my doctor.

Again (Symptom Reminder); I'm hungry, have gained 10 lbs (since beginning the Leptin RX w/huge amounts of coconut oil), tired, irritated, depressed, antisocial, no motivation, poor work ethic (unless I get a proper cup of coffee-which has stopped working) no libido, chronic constipation (going on for nearly 2 years-barley responds to laxatives anymore), muscle weakness, heart palpitations, been getting cold lately, and sometimes really warm, again no energy, gaining back weight after loosing 100lbs on low calorie ketoginic diet (Atkins style-modified to paleo with less protein more fat).

Last labs came back showing Ferritin at 417 (range: 30-400), Total T3 was low at 0.51 (1.3-3.1 range), TSH was 1.55 mIU/L (0.27-4.2), Free T4 17.0 pmol/L (12-22), blood cortisol was 694.9 nmol/L (no range-sry) fasting.

Supplements: 2000+ mg biomagnesium, 10000mg tumeric, Artichoke extract, Neem leaf powder, digestive enzyme (full spec, w/peppermint oil), Oxbile, Full spec probiotics, 30 grams FOS, antioxandant support supp, no-flush niacin, folic acid, b complex, multivit, omega 3 fish oil supp, 5000iu vit D3, 1200 vit E, 150mg selenium, vit c w/bilberry extract, BioPQQ (w. Lcarnitine, LCarnosine, TMG, NAC, etc.), Reservation, coQ10, Rholdia extract, Kelp, 1g cinnamon w/200mcg chromium, and Alpha lipolic acid. Also add Himalayan pink sea salt to my food (5+ grams daily). Nearly 100% of my protein comes from Wild salmon, and my fat from coconut oil (very large amounts). Carbs are minimal coming from coconut flour, raw cocoa powder, avocados, salad greens, mushrooms, fiber supplements, etc.

How should I approach my doctor?

Thanks for your help!

In CHRIST,
Lucas Richard

### HoneyChild — 2013-06-17T17:15:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bio-hacking-what-to-ask-for.7368/post-88245>

I don't have much to suggest but a lot of people find folic acid difficult to take because of certain genetic issues. Methylfolate (Metafolin by Solgar) is the best. Many people also avoid B-complexes with folic acid.

### Shijin13 — 2013-06-18T23:21:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bio-hacking-what-to-ask-for.7368/post-88373>

> [LRichard12 said:](/goto/post?id=88231)
>
> Dear all;
>
> I have posted this as a question for Dr. Kruse, but would also appreciate any advice from others if yo're so inclined;
>
> I know you can't practice med online, but could you help me to talk to my doc. He knows nothing about thyroid, depression, leptin, etc. but is willing to write any prescription or do any blood work I ask for (good doctor ehe).
>
> What tests should I request for my problem.
>
> Any suggested method of treatment I could discuss with my doctor.
>
> Again (Symptom Reminder); I'm hungry, have gained 10 lbs (since beginning the Leptin RX w/huge amounts of coconut oil), tired, irritated, depressed, antisocial, no motivation, poor work ethic (unless I get a proper cup of coffee-which has stopped working) no libido, chronic constipation (going on for nearly 2 years-barley responds to laxatives anymore), muscle weakness, heart palpitations, been getting cold lately, and sometimes really warm, again no energy, gaining back weight after loosing 100lbs on low calorie ketoginic diet (Atkins style-modified to paleo with less protein more fat).
>
> Last labs came back showing Ferritin at 417 (range: 30-400), Total T3 was low at 0.51 (1.3-3.1 range), TSH was 1.55 mIU/L (0.27-4.2), Free T4 17.0 pmol/L (12-22), blood cortisol was 694.9 nmol/L (no range-sry) fasting.
>
> Supplements: 2000+ mg biomagnesium, 10000mg tumeric, Artichoke extract, Neem leaf powder, digestive enzyme (full spec, w/peppermint oil), Oxbile, Full spec probiotics, 30 grams FOS, antioxandant support supp, no-flush niacin, folic acid, b complex, multivit, omega 3 fish oil supp, 5000iu vit D3, 1200 vit E, 150mg selenium, vit c w/bilberry extract, BioPQQ (w. Lcarnitine, LCarnosine, TMG, NAC, etc.), Reservation, coQ10, Rholdia extract, Kelp, 1g cinnamon w/200mcg chromium, and Alpha lipolic acid. Also add Himalayan pink sea salt to my food (5+ grams daily). Nearly 100% of my protein comes from Wild salmon, and my fat from coconut oil (very large amounts). Carbs are minimal coming from coconut flour, raw cocoa powder, avocados, salad greens, mushrooms, fiber supplements, etc.
>
> How should I approach my doctor?
>
> Thanks for your help!
>
> In CHRIST,
> Lucas Richard

Richard

How long have you been doing the LR? weight gain while on it can be a sign of hormonal imbalances. Additionally, your chronic constipation tells me you're dehydrated. as for all the supplements - is there any specific reason why you're taking all the supplement? Supplements have their place - but you may not need all your taking. Additionally, you're eating salmon which is a good seafood. If you can you really want to eat shellfish - oysters, clams, mussles, shrimps, crabs, etc...

as for labs here's where you want to start you need a lipid panel, a full thyroid panel, a full hormone panel, CBC, Metabolic panel, cortisol, dhea-s IGF-1, shbg, pregnenolone, Vit D3 - 25 Hydroxy and Magnesium RBC.

Take the list in below - tell you doc you want to get a base line of labs - to see where your at. and then you want to retest, as you continue to improve your health, to see where your numbers are. your labs are a METRIC of your health, when you look at them together! this lets you take ownership and responsibility for your health. Your doc is going to help you figure out how to read the labs - and it may be an education for both of you.

I've listed the labs my Doc Just ran for me, along w/ the CPT Codes and Test #s for LABCORP.

Berkley heart lab, or a NMR, or a VAP - these are cholesterol labs - that look at the small subfractions of the LDL (Berkley is the new standard -HsCRP, HbA1C are included in the lab along w/a bunch of other good stuff) here's a link that you can take in to your doc - to explain why you want a berkley [http://www.bhlinc.com/patients/why-berkeley/](http://www.bhlinc.com/patients/why-berkeley/)

Fasting blood glucose Test # 001818, CPT Code 82947
HsCRP
HbA1C
CBC Test # 005009, CPT Code 85025
Comp Metabolic Panel Test # 322000, CPT code 80053
Thyroid:
TSH, Test # 004259, CPT Code 84443
Free T4, Test # 001974, CPT code 84439
Free T3, Test # 010389, CTP Code 84481
RT3, Test # 070104, CPT 84482
Progestrone, Test # 004312, CPT Code 84144
Estradiol, Sensitive, Test # 140244, CPT Code 82670
SHGB Test #082016 CPT Code: 84270
Testosterone Free Direct, & serum with Total Testosterone: Test # 140103, CPT Codes 84402, 84403
Prolactin Test # 004465, CPT code 84146
cortisol Test # 004051, CPT Code 82533
DHEA-S Test # 004020, CPT Code 82627
IGF-1 Test #010636, CPT Code 84305
RBC magnesium Test # 080283, CPT Code 83735
Vitamin D25 Hyrdoxy Test # 081950, CPT code 82306
Serotonin Test# 120204, CPT Code 84260 (requires frozen specimens)
Pregnenolone Test# 140707, CPT Code 84140 (requires Frozen Specimens)

let me know if you have any questions...

I would also verify with your insurance if some more unusual tests are covered on your insurance plan since it might be cheaper to get them on your own. It happened to me that my Homocysteine test was not covered. I had to pay the lab out of pocket what they charged my insurance - a really inflated rate of $228.00. If I went to get it privately I would have paid only $70.00.

Dear Shjin,

Thank you very much for your answer! I will use this a s a basis for talking to my doctor.

I have been doing LR for about 3 weeks now. I too suspect hormonal imbalances and do look forward to getting these tests done.

As for dehydration; I drink ample if not copious amounts of water during the day and do urinate a lot. 99% of the water that I drink is filtered. I even carry a water bottle with a built in filter. I do ensure that I get an adequate potassium/magnesium/sodium (from sea salt) intake. I have had this constipation now for 2 years and it gets progressively worse. My fatigue, heart palpitations, low motivation, and depression began about around early 2010. The constipation started in late 2010, and really got bad in 2011.

My supplemental regimen is a result of reading up on hypothyroidism (which I'm sure is part of my problem), adrenal fatigue, leptin rsistance, leaky gut, etc. My goal through supplementation is to fight inflammation (which is part of my problem), support my thyroid, fight my chronic constipation, improve energy levels, and aid in weight loss.

Thanks again for the lab suggestions! I will see about getting these done asap. I can't get an appointment with the endocrinologist until the end of July, but can see if the regular GP will run these labs for me.

Thanks again for your help!

# Thread: N=1; One Week BioHack at the beach
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/n-1-one-week-biohack-at-the-beach.7370/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2013-06-17T19:16:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/n-1-one-week-biohack-at-the-beach.7370/post-88254>

So last week - we went down to New Bern NC - about 45mins from the beach. Cell Signals where we were staying were intermittent. I turned off the Wi-Fi in our unit for the entire duration. We spent 4 out of 5 days at the beach. talk about battery recharge. by day two of playing in the ocean, the whole family's need for water had increased tremendously, as did salt. both Chaos and Havoc took naps every day - and I'm not talking a 3-45min snooze. I'm talking 1-2hrs worth of sleeping! by the end of the week, dh and I were waking up before them! my cystic acne cleared up after the first day of just walking in the ocean! we ate fresh seafood every day for at least one meal. There were some days, that food held no appeal. any food we ate was light. we had a long drive back on Saturday to reality. Sunday, DH and I were so energized - we knocked out a bunch of work we've been ignoring in the house for much. Its like we had more energy than we knew what to do with.

I feel so refreshed. I'm back at work, and I've got a ton of stuff to clear through, and I'm not even stressed about it. all those electrons from the beach helped reset me for this week... looking fwd to my next trip to the beach for some camping in mid September....

### nonchalant — 2013-06-17T22:50:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/n-1-one-week-biohack-at-the-beach.7370/post-88272>

Sounds like a marvelous vacation, Gretchen. The ocean really seems to kick skin problems to the curb, but you might see them return in a few weeks, unfortunately.
Mmmm, a week at the beach... sounds like heaven. Glad you and your family were able to seize the opportunity!

### fitness@home — 2013-06-17T23:21:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/n-1-one-week-biohack-at-the-beach.7370/post-88276>

Your vacation sounds wonderful Gretchen. A little envy going on here

Isn't it amazing how it recharges your batteries?

### Hope — 2013-06-18T01:48:34+0300

Gretchen, that is awesome!! Thanks for sharing...may I ask....did you get sun burned at all? The kids? Did you have any kind of protection? Like shade? I realize it's your n=1 but I wonder......

SO excited for you!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/n-1-one-week-biohack-at-the-beach.7370/post-88302>

As often as I can I drive 2.5 hours to spend time at my grandmothers on Chincoteague Island Va...i spend all day at the beach and eat lots of fresh salty oysters and fish...i sleep like a bear at nite and i feel so amazing while im there..the beach is the best place to be to rejuvinate your energy!!

### Huck — 2013-06-19T17:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/n-1-one-week-biohack-at-the-beach.7370/post-88431>

When I lived in N VA, I used to make several trips a year to the Outer Banks. In talking with some of my friends, they all shared the same experience.

It takes a couple of days to relax and by the end of the week, life is good. No arguing, no fighting, just relaxed good fun. But once you get to Fredericksburg on the trip home, watch out. Traffic is a nightmare with people weaving in and out at 80 mph. You white knuckle the rest of the trip. By the time you finally make it home, a good argument usually erupts.

One of the best things I ever did was get the heck out of DC. The money is seductive, but the stress is unbearable.

### Trey625 — 2013-06-19T17:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/n-1-one-week-biohack-at-the-beach.7370/post-88432>

I agree Huck...Im planning my escape...my youngest daughter has 1 year left in high school then im getting outta this area...wanna head to the country within driving distance to the ocean...that is my dream : )

# Thread: How in the heck does one lower Reverse T3 levels?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-in-the-heck-does-one-lower-reverse-t3-levels.7371/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bennyha — 2013-06-17T19:38:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-in-the-heck-does-one-lower-reverse-t3-levels.7371/post-88256>

I just got another Free T3 and Rev T3 test done to see if I have made any progress over the past 6 months. I have done everything "right" during this time period. I haven't had one drop of alcohol, haven't partied, havent eaten anything bad, stayed up late (always am in bed by 9:15 at the latest), etc. I have completely given up my life and all forms of fun in order to get better. Been doing Leptin reset, circadian rhythms, CT on and off, etc. I have been doing all this stuff for almost 2 years now. Started iodine protocol again about 1 month ago.

My latest results revealed my Reverse T3 levels dropped a whopping .1, from 17.1 to 17.0 ng/dL. While the range is 9.2-24.1, my ratio is still 14, which is considered bad. It is up from 11.1, so at least I am making some small progress there. Also, Free T3 just up from 1.9 to 2.4 pg/ML, so while I am still low in that, at least I am now in range (2.0-4.4).

So my question remains: how in the heck does one lower Reverse T3 levels? And how to improve ratio?? And when will I be able to sweat again?

\-more iodine? (currently at 50 mg per day)-->considering upping to 100 mg dose

\-more carbs in diet (I have been eating less than 50 g carbs/day for a long time, and most of them are broccoli with a few carrots)-->I saw an article written by Dr. Holtorff where he says eating low carb for too long can make too much RT3 and cause troubles. But Jack says no to carbs for people needing to recover. So I am once again confused here?

\-straight T3 pills? not sure if this is a real long-term solution or more of a band-aid approach?

I was wondering if anyone actually has success or advice on how to go about this. I have given up everything in the last 2 years in order to try and get feeling good, but I have made little progress compared to the amount of time and sacrifice I put in to this. I am only 31 years old, and want to live again. Any advice would be so greatly appreciated guys

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-17T19:39:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-in-the-heck-does-one-lower-reverse-t3-levels.7371/post-88257>

next blog should open some eyes on that........

### Shijin13 — 2013-06-17T19:44:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-in-the-heck-does-one-lower-reverse-t3-levels.7371/post-88258>

Benny - you need to figure out what's driving your RT3. once you've identified why you have RT3 you can go about fixing the root cause, which will address the the RT3.

once I started treating my Hypothyroidism, w/Armour - I was able to fix my RT3 in 3 months. I was low carb during this phase even though it was summer.

What do your other labs look like? that may be telling us what's going on too. You should note that you are seeing progress... you're ratio is closer to 20 than it was before... you're getting there. This doesn't happen over night, it took me 6mths, after we figured out what was causing the RT3, and started treating the hypothyroidism....

Gretchen thank you for taking the time to respond! I have no idea what is causing the Reverse T3. I originally thought it was fungus or candida or somthing like that, but that was all ruled out. I just know I feel really bad and my hormones are all low across the board. I didn't get an extensive panel done just a few, and my testosterone was low. I had low free t4, free t3, and a high tsh (almost a 4.0). So I have thyroid problems obviously, I just don't know what is causing them.

I have both T3 cytomel and dessicated thyroid as well at my disposal, and maybe taking thyroid medication would speed up the process. I have tried seemingly everything the past 18 months, and have made so little progress. My inability to sweat, no libido, and chronic fatigue are really wearing on me.

That is interesting you fixed your RT3 with the Armour. Do you think the Armour sped things up? I was under the assumption that anything with T4 in it would make it harder for the RT3 to clear and cause more problems.

### Trinity — 2013-06-18T03:41:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-in-the-heck-does-one-lower-reverse-t3-levels.7371/post-88290>

Hi Benney,

I also had a horrible free t3 to rt3 ratio ( ration was around 7 at its worst with a rt3 over 50 )! Of course conventional docs told me everything was "fine" until I finally felt so crappy I wouldn't take the bull\*\*\*\* anymore and found a good endocrinologist. I had to take pure cytomel (t3) and it took me over a year to get up to an effective dose due to chronic adrenal fatigue and low cortisol, but my rt3 ratio is now stable around 30. I wish I knew how to do it naturally, but I took up an hour a day meditation practice and tried to eliminate every source of physical and mental stress in my life I could think of to no avail. The cytomel, however, did work. Incidentally, I also have a family history of hypothyroidism.

\--Trinity

### bennyha — 2013-06-18T20:31:14+0300

### Trinity — 2013-06-19T01:28:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-in-the-heck-does-one-lower-reverse-t3-levels.7371/post-88376>

Hi Benney,

Yes, I definitely feel l better after having improved my rt3 ratio. But with that said I have to be honest with you. Fixing my thyroid did not prove to be a "magic bullet" for me. Even after improving my thyroid function, I have continued to struggle with adrenal fatigue, insomnia, and estrogen dominance. I have made a lot of headway, but I still do not feel "great"... But at least now I do not spend half of my work day on the couch

Because you mention dealing with fatigue, I also have to warn you. Due to the complex relationship between the thyroid, adrenals, and HPA axis, it is very possible for thyroid medication to "backfire" on you. This happened to me several times and resulted in adrenal crashes with severe insomnia that took literally months to recover from. Unless you have a very good doctor, he / she probably won't know enough to warn you about this risk or recognize it when it occurs. However, because of this I had to go \*extremely\* slow with the t3 meds.

When I started I could not even tolerate 2.5 mcg of cytomel even though I was very hypothyroid. I actually began with 1.25 mcg (1/4 of the smallest pill they make) and slowly increased by quarter pills until I was up to around 10 mcg. After that, I was able to increase by around 2.5 mcg (half pills) until I cleared the rt3. Everybody is different, but I cleared my rt3 at a dose of around 40 mcg of cytomel. The process of building up to a high enough cytomel dose to clear the rt3 took me more than 1 full year and was a strenuous exercise in patience. If I moved too fast my adrenals and sleep would tank and it would take me weeks (and sometimes months) to get back to making forward progress. If you have healthy adrenal function, this process may not be as difficult for you as it was for me. As I got to higher doses (over 20 mcg), I asked my endocrinologist to write me prescriptions to test t3 and rt3 every month rather than every 3 months. Given my rather delicate situation, monitoring every 3 months was not adequate for me. (you do not want to "overshoot" if your adrenals and HPA axis is not in good working order .

In spite of aggressive monitoring, the clearing process still resulted in me becoming "hyper" for a brief period of time and this crashed my adrenals again. As a result, I had to back down to around 30 mcg of cytomel. I am still on a maintenance dose of 30 mcg of cytomel, but my rt3 ratio has been stable for about 9 months now (ratio around 30). Although my ratio is very good with a very low rt3, my free t3 level is still not optimal. My optimal dose of cytomel is probably around 35 - 37.5 mcg, but I have stayed at 30 mcg due to ongoing adrenal fatigue (not as bad as it used to be, but not gone either). My current dosing strategy is to split my dose 3 ways. I take one third upon waking, one third at noon, and the final third late afternoon. Because t3 spikes rapidly after taking cytomel, dividing the dose helps to level things out and reduce adrenal stress / demand for cortisol. I do not currently foresee getting off thyroid meds. I have labwork showing I have been hypothyroid for almost 10 years (albeit undiagnosed for much of that time). Your case, however, may be different.

I should also mention that it was necessary for me to take Cortef 15-20mg per day in order to initially tolerate the cytomel, however, over the past 9 months I have been able to very slowly wean off through the use of pregnenolone and near daily CT baths. My cortisol still is not great, but I am functioning reasonably "OK" and still tolerating the cytomel. My opinion on Cortef is that it was necessary for me at the time (to tolerate enough cytomel to clear the rt3), but that it did not fix the underlying problems in the long run. Although I am not a doctor, my personal research and experience support's Dr. K's theory that "adrenal fatigue" is more of a brain signaling problem (brain not sending the right signals to make the right amounts of cortisol at the right time of day) rather than glandular "fatigue", per se.

Hope this helps,
Trinity

To adjust thyroid hormones one should have first
Circadian Rhythm in order
good morning saliva cortisol
selenium & Lugols
\----
Not much sense using Cytomel in effort to remove RT3.
Better goal is
FreeT3 in upper 1/4 of laboratory range
if required use thyroid extract from pigs, daily, in the morning, chew it,
on day of blood test, draw blood first, take thyroid hormone after blood draw
\----
Instead of adrenal extract (that is a black box of unknown content), get
Spectracell Micronutrient Analysis
spectracell.com
all adrenal and thyroid items are there and much more
\-----
Good idea to use pregnenolone rather than progesterone in effort to optimize
cholesterol-pregnenolone-progesterone-cortisol chain
but
get DHEAs first
goal
Women 275-400 μg/dL
take as many as it takes DHEA pills, I buy mine from amazon.com, made by lef.org
had a good luck using
pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix 150mg tabs, from Nutricology
goal #1 cortisol(7;30AM)=~20ug/dL
goal #2 (on 2-4 day of cycle) progesterone=1.5ng/mL
goal #3 (on 19-21 day of cycle) progesterone=(15-20)
if goal1 is ok but goal2 is behind, some progesterone may be added in those times)
goal #4 (post menapausal) progesterone (15-20)ng/mL
\-----------------
get:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-4#post-199890](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-4#post-199890)

Redo ICD-10 codes (and let me have them)
get most or all on that list.
be fasted at 7AM at LabCorp

I have given up everything in the last 2 years in order to try and get feeling good, but I have made little progress compared to the amount of time and sacrifice I put in to this. I am only 31 years old, and want to live again. Any advice would be so greatly appreciated guys

> [bennyha said:](/goto/post?id=88256)
>
> I just got another Free T3 and Rev T3 test done to see if I have made any progress over the past 6 months. I have done everything "right" during this time period. I haven't had one drop of alcohol, haven't partied, havent eaten anything bad, stayed up late (always am in bed by 9:15 at the latest), etc. I have completely given up my life and all forms of fun in order to get better. Been doing Leptin reset, circadian rhythms, CT on and off, etc. I have been doing all this stuff for almost 2 years now. Started iodine protocol again about 1 month ago.
>
> My latest results revealed my Reverse T3 levels dropped a whopping .1, from 17.1 to 17.0 ng/dL. While the range is 9.2-24.1, my ratio is still 14, which is considered bad. It is up from 11.1, so at least I am making some small progress there. Also, Free T3 just up from 1.9 to 2.4 pg/ML, so while I am still low in that, at least I am now in range (2.0-4.4).
>
> So my question remains: how in the heck does one lower Reverse T3 levels? And how to improve ratio?? And when will I be able to sweat again?
>
> \-more iodine? (currently at 50 mg per day)-->considering upping to 100 mg dose
>
> \-more carbs in diet (I have been eating less than 50 g carbs/day for a long time, and most of them are broccoli with a few carrots)-->I saw an article written by Dr. Holtorff where he says eating low carb for too long can make too much RT3 and cause troubles. But Jack says no to carbs for people needing to recover. So I am once again confused here?
>
> \-straight T3 pills? not sure if this is a real long-term solution or more of a band-aid approach?
>
> I was wondering if anyone actually has success or advice on how to go about this. I have given up everything in the last 2 years in order to try and get feeling good, but I have made little progress compared to the amount of time and sacrifice I put in to this. I am only 31 years old, and want to live again. Any advice would be so greatly appreciated guys

Hi Benny,
It has been awhile since you posted the comment about your frustrations with getting op top of your RT3 problem. I am much the same as you. I am trying everything I can to get my RT3 ration down. I have been advised by my natural doctors (osteopath and a Chinese herbalist/acupuncturist) that finding the root cause as to why my RT3 is high is the primary goal. Maybe its insulin or leptin resistance, Lymes disease, poor Iron conversion. I am doing more tests this week.
As far as diet goes I was advised to cut down on Goitrogenic foods (broccoli is one) for more optimal thyroid function. I did this and after one month my T3 was in top range.
I am getting plenty of sunlight. That's easy down here in Oz.
How are you doing now? I hope all is good. Steve

# Thread: Epi-Paleo Backpacking
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-backpacking.7373/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2013-06-17T22:27:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-backpacking.7373/post-88269>

We have started backpacking in the Sierra Nevada and find trying to stay Epi-Paleo with the constraints of carrying everything a real challenge. Would love to hear if anyone else has faced this and what you can recommend. We were out for a 2 day/1 night this past weekend, and the food wasn't too much problem, but we're planning a week trip later this summer. Suggestions?

As an aside, I don't seem to have permission to post in Ask Jack. How to fix?

Tom

### nonchalant — 2013-06-17T22:41:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-backpacking.7373/post-88270>

Hi Tom, I'd put in a support ticket. Check the jackkruse.com website for clues on that. I think there is a link somewhere to put in a ticket.

Are sardines and other canned seafood too heavy? I imagine it would be a huge challenge. Oh, I once got some dried anchovies in salt at the oriental market. If you are lucky you could find some without MSG.

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2013-06-17T22:47:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-backpacking.7373/post-88271>

Yes, cans are too heavy. As it was for our overnight, My pack weighed upwards 40 to 50 lbs. We had hard boiled eggs for breakfast and dehdrated grass fed beef stew for dinner. Definitely looking for ideas.

### nonchalant — 2013-06-17T22:53:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-backpacking.7373/post-88273>

This looks something like what I found, though I know I only paid about a third of this price at the market.

[http://www.amazon.com/Shirakiku-Iri...r\_1\_1?s=grocery&ie=UTF8&qid=1371498801&sr=1-1](http://www.amazon.com/Shirakiku-Iriko-Cooked-Anchovy-6-ounce/dp/B00BA73UQ8/ref=sr_1_1?s=grocery&ie=UTF8&qid=1371498801&sr=1-1)

### cinnamon — 2013-06-17T23:39:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-backpacking.7373/post-88277>

The clerk at my local seafood store mentioned she likes to take fresh oysters together with her shucking knife when she goes for an impromptu hike/picnic. Even with no cooler, maybe you could tuck them in around frozen water containers and then feast on them the first day?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-18T03:56:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-backpacking.7373/post-88295>

seaweed, dried fish jerky from the Korena markets, dried beef or venison jerky........and water found on a steam boiled on a fire........sunlight on the skin.......touching lots of trees. Smelling nature. Nature is where it is.

### Kristin M — 2013-06-23T07:01:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-backpacking.7373/post-88715>

Take a fishing pole....lots of rainbow trout in the Sierra! I've sufficed in the past with 'found' line and lure along the river and a stick. Very satisfying to catch a fish with free equipment?

Kristin M

# Thread: Women: How to Increase DHEA w/o Increasing Testosterone
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-how-to-increase-dhea-w-o-increasing-testosterone.7376/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Trinity — 2013-06-18T03:32:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-how-to-increase-dhea-w-o-increasing-testosterone.7376/post-88287>

Hi Guys,

Last time I posted for help on labs, JansZ suggested that I try the MLM DHEA and pregnenolone from nutricology. Thanks JansZ! The cortisol is still a work in progress (trying to complete a wean off Cortef), but I have seen substantial progress in my DHEA level. The issue now seems to be that it's converting into too much testosterone. Here are the results of 2 salivary DHEA / testosterone tests. For reference, my DHEA level was at the very bottom of the reference range prior to trying the MLM formulation of DHEA.

TEST 1: 4/21/13 (50mg MLM DHEA, 100mg MLM pregnenolone, 25 mg 7-keto DHEA)

DHEAS (saliva): 12.5 ng/ml (2-23)
Testosterone (saliva): 360 pg/ml (16-55) yikes!

After this test, I reduced DHEA to 37.5 mg, pregnenolone to 75 mg, and eliminated the 7-keto. The results ~6 weeks later were:

TEST 2: 6/4/13 (37.5 mg MLM DHEA, 75 mg MLM pregnenolone )

DHEAS (saliva): 11.2 ng/ml (2-23)
Testosterone (saliva): 236 pg/ml (16-55) reduced, but still too high

I probably need to reduce the DHEA further because it is converting into too much testosterone, but after finally seeing progress I also don't want to tank my DHEA again. How does a woman actually get DHEA to the top of the reference range while maintaining a normal T level? I realize that 7-keto is an option for women, but after having followed some of the posts on this forum it appears like it may not be very effective at raising DHEA on labs. For this reason, I've stuck primarily to regular DHEA. I am thinking maybe I should reduce DHEA to 25mg for a month or so and retest. Any thoughts?

Incidentally, in spite of extremely high T levels for a couple of months, I have not observed any major side effects yet (excess hair growth, acne, ect).

Thanks,
Trinity

### JanSz — 2013-06-18T20:04:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-how-to-increase-dhea-w-o-increasing-testosterone.7376/post-88357>

> [Trinity said:](/goto/post?id=88287)
>
> Hi Guys,
>
> **Last time I posted for help on labs,** JansZ suggested that I try the MLM DHEA and pregnenolone from nutricology. Thanks JansZ! The cortisol is still a work in progress (trying to complete a wean off Cortef), but I have seen substantial progress in my DHEA level. The issue now seems to be that it's converting into too much testosterone. Here are the results of 2 salivary DHEA / testosterone tests. For reference, my DHEA level was at the very bottom of the reference range prior to trying the MLM formulation of DHEA.
>
> TEST 1: 4/21/13 (50mg MLM DHEA, 100mg MLM pregnenolone, 25 mg 7-keto DHEA)
>
> DHEAS (saliva): 12.5 ng/ml (2-23)
> Testosterone (saliva): 360 pg/ml (16-55) yikes!
>
> After this test, I reduced DHEA to 37.5 mg, pregnenolone to 75 mg, and eliminated the 7-keto. The results ~6 weeks later were:
>
> TEST 2: 6/4/13 (37.5 mg MLM DHEA, 75 mg MLM pregnenolone )
>
> DHEAS (saliva): 11.2 ng/ml (2-23)
> Testosterone (saliva): 236 pg/ml (16-55) reduced, but still too high
>
> I probably need to reduce the DHEA further because it is converting into too much testosterone, but after finally seeing progress I also don't want to tank my DHEA again. How does a woman actually get DHEA to the top of the reference range while maintaining a normal T level? I realize that 7-keto is an option for women, but after having followed some of the posts on this forum it appears like it may not be very effective at raising DHEA on labs. For this reason, I've stuck primarily to regular DHEA. I am thinking maybe I should reduce DHEA to 25mg for a month or so and retest. Any thoughts?
>
> **Incidentally, in spite** of extremely high T levels for a couple of months, **I have not observed any major side effects yet (excess hair growth, acne, ect).**
>
> Thanks,
> Trinity

Consider staying on just one thread, it will be easier to review past communications.
If you choose to do that,
please copy and re-post here our communications from other places.

Consider doing mostly blood and urine analysis.
Reserve saliva for cortisol (if that, but good to get initial impression and latter, fine tuning)).

You may want to do those tests at LabCorp.
Ask your doctor for script for items that you want to review,
you may want to pick them from list here:
[http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508)

If indeed you have high testosterone, may want to investigate for
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PCOS](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PCOS)

The testosterone may also raise when taking medications that are
5α-reductase inhibitors
Aromatase inhibitors
In
that case testosterone may raise because it is (not used) not down-metabolized, (so it is not because you are making too much of it)

.....

# Thread: We fight every day.... But I need to cure my younger brother!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-fight-every-day-but-i-need-to-cure-my-younger-brother.7378/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-18T04:56:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-fight-every-day-but-i-need-to-cure-my-younger-brother.7378/post-88304>

Please Read: [http://paleohacks.com/questions/170915/help-me-cure-aid-my-younger-brother-please#axzz2WWiB4ram](http://paleohacks.com/questions/170915/help-me-cure-aid-my-younger-brother-please#axzz2WWiB4ram)

This is an old post I made. Things have gotten worse. I need to help and I'm hoping I can get a whole bunch of people to basically support what I've suggested and eventually my mom and brother will listen.

WARNING! Some Graphic Info contained!

Corrections To Post:
(1) Brother is no longer taking norco's for pain, they're now giving him OxyCodone - he is 14yrs old. He takes 4-6 a day I believe. Still on all the other medications.
(2) No longer has tubes in his anus to drain pus.
(3) He essentially has a large hole between his anus and where his scrotum meet. It is deep. Not only is it a large deep hole, but there are sores and cavities in this hole - essentially there are hollow areas in this hole, think of it like tunnels running inside of this large open wound. My mother must go in there every day and pack it with gawes and put chemicals on them. She needs to shove it far in there with something that looks like a large chopstick that is at least 2ft long. He has a nurse that comes to the house occasionally as well.

Additional Info:
(1) Allergies to the following: Tree nuts but not peanuts; fish but not shellfish and crustaceans; many seeds (pumpkin); possibly coconut products - it is a nut, no, or a fruit? I don't know, lol. He is allergic to many medications as well. Keep in mind these are extreme allergies, nothing like slight swelling or an itchy roof of the mouth. They are the allergies that can cause death.
(2) He has been on antibiotics many times before. He is not currently, but I believe they may have drastically altered his gut flora, and considering his inflammatory diseases, this is not good.
(3) He is constantly surrounded by stress (emotional/mental) and EMF
The unfortunate caveat here is that he really doesn't move from his position on the sofa. He plays a lot of video games online with friends that he has made on them. They are actually good friends even though they have never met in real life. They have purchased him video games and accompany him from afar on his surgeries and await for him to get back and game with them. What I'm getting at is he plays lots of video games, but there is not much else he can do. He can't go for walks or exercise or go outside of the house for long periods of time. I don't blame him for wanting to get entertainment through online gaming and the socialization that occurs. This is bad, though, in that he constantly wears a head set, is in close vicinity to a non-grounded computer, and has artificial lights surrounding him all night and day. His sleep is terrible as well. He pulls all nighters, will get on a good sleep schedule, and then will do the same. Sometimes he wakes up at 7am and falls asleep at 10-11pm for a week, then it will literally be the opposite of that. Then sometimes he will just be up for 24-30hrs. Not good for recovery.

I personally believe I know, or at least have ideas as to what can help him on this road to recovery. The thing is, I don't think my mom nor brother believe everything I say outside of eating a paleo-style diet, which they both don't do. I'm hoping that if a bunch of people comment and state everything that should be done, and how confident they are it will make a difference, they may be encouraged to make a change if I print out this page and post it in the living room. I'm also completely naive to various pharmaceutical treatments that may be necessary. I've heard good things about Hyperbaric oxygen pressure therapy for Crohn's and other inflammatory conditions.

There is some other additional information that is needed to make things clear:
(1) We have no money. I don't mean we live paycheck to paycheck - we live day to day. Luckily I just landed a new job at McDonald's and they are in fact training me as a manager which = college credits and something good to put on my resume. I will be able to start up BJJ again and buy seafood as well as pay for my car, my family doesn't see things that way. They buy TV dinners, frozen pizzas, chips, etc. I've shown them that I've in fact found ways to eat healthily for a cheap budget - my seafood often costs less than their chips and snacks (less than $2 for a pound of fish if its canned, and 4 dollars for 2lbs of mussels frozen, as opposed to $2-4 for a bag of chips that doesn't fill anyone up). Even if they didn't go on a paleo or epi-paleo diet, I think that it would be wise to buy MEALS. Stop buying frozen food and make that crap themselves. Stop buying soda and water bottles, and regardless of the fluoride drink tap water for now. Save every penny.
(2) My brother seems to believe heavily in drinking Sprite when he has an episode. It soothes him and slightly eases the pain. Are there any alternatives to this? He also drinks/eats hot soup that is typically the thicker, heavier, creamier kind as it supposedly soothes him. I believe there has to be alternatives. He also claims that if he ever takes his Oxycodone on an empty stomach, he has an attack, therefore he eats every time he takes this. Any snack ideas for this? I downloaded paleo cookbooks and I found recipes for salsa, guacamole, "hummus", and so on, all that can be eaten as snacks, but he seems to believe this is impossible and needs to warm up a hot pocket to eat with his meds.
(3) We live a high stress lifestyle. We are completely broke and unfortunately get a LINK card monthly for food, and that barely covers it because we get little. My mom hates that we have to do this - we've struggled since I've been born and have never accepted help from the government, but it's too difficult now. Literally NO ONE works at my house (although I do now). My mom has to be on call for my brother 24/7 and thus cannot work. My dad, well, I don't know what his excuse is. I could write a book about all this crap, but my dad is not stepping up to the plate. One year he made something around 8,000 dollars....... He works less now. It's terrible. There is fights every single day - violent fights. So much more. Essentially, it is extremely tense and stress is abundant. If it wasn't for my fiance I don't know where I'd be (I have my own set of symptoms that I'm battling).

What I believe is necessary:
(1)At the minimum cut out fast food, soda, and processed meals. There are studies that show a direct link between these foodstuffs and autoimmune disorders. Make the foods they love from scratch and buy in bulk from Sams/Costco. Ideally I'd want him and my mom to be on at least a paleo diet, but I don't see it moving in that direction. So at the minimum completely eliminate these processed "foodstuffs"... ahem, poison.
(2) Convince my brother that at the minimum he needs to have a consistent sleep schedule. I told him that it's terrible for him to be doing this, but even if he were to want to stay up late and game, make sure that he is waking up at the same time give or take some amount of time (30-1hr) every single day. He has to time his 6MP, one dose per 24hrs, and his dosing gets screwed up as it is from this. Awaken at more consistent times, take medications and consistent times, and ideally eat at consistent times
(3) Take Cold showers. I just don't know how this would affect his medications - could it increase the potency? If not I think that taking cold showers and spot icing every day, especially the open hole/wound could potentially have drastic effects
(4) Incorporate at least weekly shellfish
(5) Damn.... So much more

I'm hoping the wise, intelligent, and thoughtful members at this board and/or Dr. Kruse can help me convince my mother and brother that these are necessary steps or else HE WILL **NEVER** get better and my in fact get **WORSE**. Thanks to anyone who will help me.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-18T05:02:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-fight-every-day-but-i-need-to-cure-my-younger-brother.7378/post-88306>

Low dose Naltrexone, HBOT, and lots of CT..........are a few things rolling in my mind.

Fixing the gut flora is tied to gut flora, the photoperiod, and the Vitamin A cycle in his brain and gut.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-18T05:23:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-fight-every-day-but-i-need-to-cure-my-younger-brother.7378/post-88311>

Interesting, I've heard about Low Dose Naltrexone. I will look further into that. I'm certain HBOT would help, I just don't know if it is covered by our insurance. CT: is it okay with his medications? Should he time it at any certain points? Would spot icing help as well?

In terms of gut flora, the photoperiod, and Vitamin A cycle, what are your suggestions. I'm not up on all of your recent blogs but I believe you were talking about Vitamin A in either QB 8 or CPC 6; what exactly would Vitamin A have to do with his gut and what not? Would you recommend he consume any probiotics or digestive enzymes? Well, I feel that they may be of no use if he is eating grains and legumes, but maybe..? I'm hoping that this post can be the catalyst for change, in which case, I think the EpcotRx would be necessary plus/minus some things.

Thanks for your quick response, greatly appreciated.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-18T06:20:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-fight-every-day-but-i-need-to-cure-my-younger-brother.7378/post-88324>

Just working on the next blog post.......Hormone 102. And re reading this............

This popped into my head. So here you go.......I hope you insert this into the situation in the manner I intend it.

Reach for the inner reaches of your outer spaces. A sculptor finds their inner Michelangelo in a hunk of marble by asking the marble what it wants to become. Today begin to remove the soot covering you one chip at a time to reveal the exquisite you buried inside by bad thinking. Transformational change never happens by eureka discoveries, but it begins by removing the excess that blinds us. You don't find yourself.......you create yourself. Sometimes we need to embrace our 'inner crazy' for a change of pace.

When we put fences around people ability to think, we create sheep that 'we herd'. Everyone gets ideas when they sleep and dream. It’s the person who gets out of the bed, wipes the sand from their eyes, and does something about that crazy inspiration who makes a difference in life. Ideation without execution, leads to deletion of any good idea. All great ideas and thoughts usually have ridiculous beginnings. Actions insufflates ideas to life. To create, you must close your eyes and listen to the voices within.

You can not change your bro.......he has to be the artist and find his own inner masterpiece. You can however lay the tools of engagement at his feet.
The truly creative person is one who can think crazy; When someone is in the vortex of disease embracing your intuition is sometimes all you have. Such a person knows full well that many of his "great ideas" will prove to be worthless. The creative person is always flexible and rejects CW and dogma; he is able to change as the situation changes, to break habits, to face indecision and changes in conditions without undue stress. He is not threatened by the unexpected as rigid, inflexible people are.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-18T06:48:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-fight-every-day-but-i-need-to-cure-my-younger-brother.7378/post-88325>

Wow, that is quite incredible, Dr. Kruse. Of course, I believe every word of it; completely resonates with my state of being/mind. I'm trying to do what you're describing - I'm trying to better myself and go out and DO. Not just imagine and dream, but actually DO. Do what I believe in my heart is right, and do what I believe will lead to a better life and world for others. I'm trying to lead by example to my family. They have commented multiple times on how I look really good - I'm starting to get built and I'm tanning easily. My dark circles are slowly dissipating. I complain of psychological symptoms less. I'm happy and mindful. My mother has even spoken about how she would love to eat the way I eat (even though she doesn't do it, lol), and how "when she was thin" she only ate 1-2 meals a day, walked, danced and swam a lot, and ate primarily fish.

I'm trying to help lay the guide stones for my brother. Although his education has taken a toll from his illnesses, he is bright. He would love to partake in athletics again like he used to be able to (football). Also, he is a fantastic cook. He has even been contemplating going to culinary school and we have spoken about opening a restaurant together - he could be the head cook/co-owner and I'll be co-owner/manager and source high quality food. I want him to get better so badly, he could become the next top chef.... epi-paleo style!

Thanks for the kind and eye opening words, Dr. Kruse. I'm going to show this post (as well as one I made on the bulletproof exec) to my mom and my brother and hope it sets off a cascade of, no not hormones (although those will come into play as well, lol), but thoughts. Thoughts that will lead to better actions. As you always say, doc, when you know better, you do better.

### caroline — 2013-06-18T09:43:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-fight-every-day-but-i-need-to-cure-my-younger-brother.7378/post-88335>

In my own life I see that people are noticing ..... and I think just waiting to see if I hang in there and am willing/want to maintain this lifestyle. They don't get yet that this is my life ..... it isn't some "flash in the pan diet/protocol" like all the other times. This is how I am making the rest of this journey .... it is all very real and this silly grin on my face is staying there no matter what!

I now get more serious questions and they actually listen to the answers - but - they still put it in "the too hard basket" I fear. I want to shake them and yell at them and tell them ---if I can do this - anyone can!

We just have to be the best possible example and if they want more - they have to go get it for themselves. As Dr. K. has said many times ... he can't fix anyone but himself......

I wish I was smarter and could impart more information to them - but that doesn't work for Dr. K. .... why on earth would it work for me!

Could you keep chatting to your brother about wonderful food and. opening a restaurant and get him excited about the possibilities for the future
and it will give him the motivation to forge ahead and work on his many health challenges. When you have a passion for cooking/food it transcends so much......No one I know does it for the money ......it is all about love and passion. I suppose much like JK is doing this.

Who is his favorite Chef? maybe get him a new cookbook by that chef with beutiful pictures and help him cook some of the recipes .... bonding time for both of you......I guess it is all about the love in the long run...

### Destiny — 2013-06-18T14:01:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-fight-every-day-but-i-need-to-cure-my-younger-brother.7378/post-88341>

My loved one had Crohn's since his teens. It was not diagnosed until his late 20's when he had an emergency surgery to remove part of his small and large intestines. This was followed by prednisone, which calmed things down but that was not the permanent solution. So I started researching all on Crohn's. We tried many things, some were helping a bit some not at all. It was a trial and error thing. Good thing with Crohn's is you know pretty quick if it is for you or not.

Then we found this book - highly recommended:
[http://www.amazon.com/Patient-Heal-...1371552494&sr=8-4&keywords=jordan+rubin+books](http://www.amazon.com/Patient-Heal-Thyself-Remarkable-Groundbreaking/dp/1893910245/ref=sr_1_4?ie=UTF8&qid=1371552494&sr=8-4&keywords=jordan+rubin+books)

"Author Jordan Rubin, N.M.D., C.N.C., survived terminal Crohn’s disease. This story of his experience with alternative medicine and the cure that he eventually discovered in homeostatic soil organisms and the Maker’s Diet gives hope to everyone with an incurable disease. ‘No matter what health challenges plague you today, there is hope for an answer.’ So says Jordan Rubin in the introduction to this truly unique book. After all, he knows: Once at death’s door, he turned his life around. This book details how you too can make lasting changes in your life. In these pages, you will learn the health secrets that allowed our ancestors to live long, disease free lives. You will learn how to regain your health if you’ve lost it or how to maintain the excellent health that you currently enjoy and even slow premature aging. If you’re looking for an optimal health plan for you and your family that is validated by history, science and our Creator, then buckle up; you’re in for a wild ride!"

We also bought his probiotics (Primal Defense) [http://www.amazon.com/Garden-Life-D...3576&sr=1-1&keywords=primal+defense+probiotic](http://www.amazon.com/Garden-Life-Defense-Ultimate-Probiotics/dp/B000GWG8FS/ref=sr_1_1?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1371553576&sr=1-1&keywords=primal+defense+probiotic)
and other suggested supplements that brought an overdue remission to my dear one. He also suggested there a specific diet. Over the years, we observed that **the best was taking the supplements daily and not eating many carbs and certain fibers**. There were still some episodes but nothing to compare to before.

Unfortunately, he still had peri annal fistula that was permanently there and got aggravated from time to time. When, he started following Dr. K's recommendations - stricter diet, CT twice a day, the fistula completely closed/healed and is gone. We think the CT twice a day did that. I wish you and your brother patience, it takes time and a lot of effort to figure out what works and what doesn't but I think all this should markedly improve your brother's condition.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-18T18:40:11+0300

@Destiny: Thanks for the great information. I'm happy to hear of your loved ones recovery, how is he doing now? Thanks for the link to the book and probiotic supplement. I've seen that probiotic before - that is one that popped into mind as well as Bio-Kult from the women who created the GAPS diet. I feel as though he will definitely have a problem cutting out carbs - he loves them! But I need to get him in the direction of at least not eating the processed carbs. Then slide into the direction of more paleo type carbs. I don't think he understands he can still make himself pizzas and what not - I've seen many primal pizzas and other typically unhealthy treats made primal/paleo.

What really strikes me as amazing is the peri anal fistula. I'm not 100% sure if my brother still has fistulas or not (has had too many surgeries), but he still has that large, open wound/hole. If CT could make a dent in aiding fistulas, I'm sure it could at least aid in healing some of the abscesses in that wound. The fact that you just shared that story may help convince them this is needed. Thank you!

You may think you have eliminated the EMFs from your wireless modem by plugging an ethernet cable into the back of modem and into the ethernet port on your computer. But you must also turn off the software via your computer or the modem will continue to put out emfs.

This video will show how to do that.

### SeaHorse — 2013-06-20T18:57:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/june-2013-webinar.7393/post-88500>

Learning about the Default Mode Network was a real eye opener for me....I'll have a bunch of questions for the Q and A that's for sure!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-06-20T19:36:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/june-2013-webinar.7393/post-88502>

The default network is an interconnected and anatomically defined brain system that preferentially activates when individuals engage in internal tasks such as daydreaming, envisioning the future, retrieving memories, and gauging others' perspectives. You can really train the circuits with meditation. This is the circuit that Monks use to do many of the things they can.
It is negatively correlated with brain systems that focus on external visual signals but really is tied to auditory and social circuitry. It is very abnormally developed in autism spectrum disorders due to changes in retinoic acid. Its subsystems include part of the medial temporal lobe for memory, part of the medial prefrontal cortex for theory of mind, and the posterior cingulate cortex for integration, along with the adjacent ventral precuneus and the medial, lateral and inferior parietal cortex. In the infant brain, there is limited evidence of the default network, but default network connectivity is more consistent in children aged 9–12 years, suggesting that the default network undergoes developmental change.

1.Buckner, R. L.; Andrews-Hanna, J. R.; Schacter, D. L. (2008). "The Brain's Default Network: Anatomy, Function, and Relevance to Disease". Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences 1124: 1–38
2\. Zhang S, Li CS (February 2012). "Functional connectivity mapping of the human precuneus by resting state fMRI". NeuroImage 59 (4): 3548–3562.
3\. Raichle, Marcus E.; Snyder, Abraham Z. (2007). "A default mode of brain function: A brief history of an evolving idea". NeuroImage 37 (4): 1083–90.
4\. Jang, Joon Hwan; Jung, Wi Hoon; Kang, Do-Hyung; Byun, Min Soo; Kwon, Soo Jin; Choi, Chi-Hoon; Kwon, Jun Soo (2011). "Increased default mode network connectivity associated with meditation". Neuroscience Letters 487 (3): 358–62.

### SeaHorse — 2013-06-20T20:25:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/june-2013-webinar.7393/post-88508>

Would that be one of the factors explaining a whole series of increased social anxiety and hyper-selfawareness/selfconsciousness that started at age eight with DD? I remember her asking me in a very serious way why she wasn't a tree or a bush...it sounds like a funny story now, but she was already having very existential thoughts. There wasn't a lot of differentiation between her self and the outside world. She was incredibly verbal, but also almost entirely literal. Her dinosaur fascination was all about why they were no longer here, what made them disappear and then she quickly took on the fear that we could have the same fate...this was when she was six....(she probably would have loved to hear about Factor X!) She lived in her daydreams, constantly thought about the future and was hyper sensitive to what she felt others thought about her. I was teaching her to meditate to calm her mind so she could sleep at the same age....

anyway...I'll keep my other questions until tonight...

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-06-20T22:52:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/june-2013-webinar.7393/post-88525>

Wow, I need to get the funds to see this webinar. From the descriptions everyone is giving, it really hits home with me. Similar to Shijin, I experience similar psychological/physical 'symptoms' where I feel as if I'm floating, or weightless, or 'popping out of my body'. In fact, this used to occur when I would go to sleep after I started learning about lucid dreaming - I would get this feeling described by more mystical minds (people speaking about 'astral projection') where you vibrate and feel almost electrically wired. Your heart pounds, and you feel like your soul/consciousness is going to pop "out of body". I would get frightened almost, and then when I switched positioning, it would go away. Only when I lied on my back would it occur, lol. My 'migraines' were very similar. I too also many times experience some sort of separation of self. I used to look into disassociation disorders and wondered if I developed one after the events that lead me on the road to optimal, but I don't feel THAT disconnected. There are times, though, that I can easily be swayed in the direction of all is fake, or all is but a lie (reality). But then again many mystics believe this is so, maybe I'm not that far gone, lol.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcoidose-and-vitamin-d3.7396/post-88419>

Hi

I was just told by my doc that I might have Sarcoidose in my eyes and that it might be because of a too high intake of Vitamin D3!
What are your thoughts on that??? I take 5000iu D3 and 10mg K2 each day because I have oteoporosis - I am only 38 years old. The D3 and K2 is not orderd by my doc but something I have started on my own after reading the osteoporosis blogs by JC.

Not sure what to do

### nonchalant — 2013-06-19T16:32:47+0300

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcoidose-and-vitamin-d3.7396/post-88426>

Is the vitamin d3 in oil?
Dr. Kruse said when he is taking high doses of vitamin D3, he will periodically test his vision in low light. Evidently vitamin d toxicity will impair night vision.

I take 15,000 IU of D3 daily. If the doctor thinks you are taking too much, he should be able to run a quick blood test to check your levels.

### HoneyChild — 2013-06-20T13:26:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcoidose-and-vitamin-d3.7396/post-88485>

Are you taking any magnesium? Apparently it's needed to convert vitamin D into it's active form so there are concerns that if you take a lot of vitamin D you'll eventually deplete your magnesium levels. If you want to test for magnesium then you need to get an RBC (Red Blood Cell) and aim for 6.5 mg/dL. Alternatively get a hair mineral test. I live in the UK and got a hair test through [http://www.mineralcheck.com/products/mineral+test](http://www.mineralcheck.com/products/mineral+test)

Some info about vitamin D and magnesium:-

[http://drcarolyndean.com/2012/12/overdosing-vitamin-d3/](http://drcarolyndean.com/2012/12/overdosing-vitamin-d3/)
[http://drcarolyndean.com/2013/03/the-vitamin-d-debate/](http://drcarolyndean.com/2013/03/the-vitamin-d-debate/)
[http://drcarolyndean.com/2013/03/too-much-vitamin-d/](http://drcarolyndean.com/2013/03/too-much-vitamin-d/)

### RGP — 2013-06-20T14:30:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcoidose-and-vitamin-d3.7396/post-88487>

Jasmin: if you live in the US, you can get a magnesium RBC test for $49 from requestatest.com.

### Jasmin — 2013-06-21T09:08:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarcoidose-and-vitamin-d3.7396/post-88563>

Honeychild
I take 3 tablets of 1250mg magnesium malate before bedtime, but perhaps I should take more....
My level of RBC has not been tested yet, but I have a appointment with a rheumatologist in december and I will ask him to test my RBC level. I havent got much money so the price is important - I know that the hair mineral test that I can get here in denmark is out of my reach at this time

I will look at the info about vit D and magnesium you posted - thank you

# Thread: Vince Flynn Dies from Prostate Cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vince-flynn-dies-from-prostate-cancer.7397/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2013-06-19T17:46:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vince-flynn-dies-from-prostate-cancer.7397/post-88433>

I was late to the party and just started reading Vince Flynn this year. I was hooked on the 1st book I read and have read every book of his I can get my hands on.

I had read he had cancer, but had no idea how serious it was. He died this morning of stage 3 metastatic prostate cancer at the age of 47. A little over a year ago, Flynn had said his outlook was good.

> Minnesota author Vince Flynn has died after a long battle with prostate cancer.
>
> WCCO-TV has learned that Flynn died Wednesday morning at United Hospital in St. Paul.
>
> Flynn has authored 15 novels centered around the character of Mitch Rapp, an undercover CIA agent. The majority of those novels have made it to the New York Times bestseller list.

[http://minnesota.cbslocal.com/2013/06/19/mn-author-vince-flynn-dies-at-47/](http://minnesota.cbslocal.com/2013/06/19/mn-author-vince-flynn-dies-at-47/)

The article said his treatment included hormone therapy, diet (no red meat), and exercise. In another article, he talked about new drugs, but wasn't specific.

### CarolMorris — 2013-06-19T18:27:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vince-flynn-dies-from-prostate-cancer.7397/post-88434>

Very sorry to hear this. I love his novels.

# Thread: Risks of Long Haul Flights and how to mitigate
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risks-of-long-haul-flights-and-how-to-mitigate.7401/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2013-06-19T22:14:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risks-of-long-haul-flights-and-how-to-mitigate.7401/post-88446>

Hi,

I heard on a recent podcast that when you travel 20 hours on a long haul flight then it takes 20 days to normalise the body and that during that period you are in a pre-diabetic state.

Is that true? What are the dangers of long haul and what steps can I take to protect myself, wife and three young children.

I know Dr. Kruse has a protocol that he follows that includes pre-flight supplementation, grounding and post flight activities but I cannot find the exact details and I am also interested in what works for other people.

Last time we flew UK to Australia we spent 1hr during second part of the journey showering, taking spa and sauna and it made a huge difference. I have never been able to sleep on these flights due to noise and general discomfort factor (being 6ft 4") despite cocktail of Valerian root and Melatonin tablets plus own plump neck pillow. At least I know now that watching films 16+ hours on the IPAD is not a good idea! And taking own supply of structured Spring water is warranted plus fasting!

Would for example a portable Schumann resonance device be a good idea? - If it is good enough for NASA cosmonauts should be good enough for me.

Any other advice most welcome and thanks in advance.

### Hope — 2013-06-19T23:54:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risks-of-long-haul-flights-and-how-to-mitigate.7401/post-88449>

hi. I haven't flown 20 hours yet. I also haven't been able to take any of my own water, but maybe that is different where you are....so here are a few observations, i.e. my n=1

Lots of grounding is great, before and after, and drinking and exposing yourself to daylight right away, if your destination is in daylight when you get there at least. Also, sleeping in darkness before - being super strict - I believe has helped me have the best (i.e. least jet lag) ever, as well as seeing about putting bare feet on the metals on the bottom of the seat in front (or underneath you if you can reach) while in flight. This appeared to have made a huge difference for me even on a shorter flight (2 hours) I took earlier this year, though I had also gotten two hours of great sun each day beforehand, along with the other stuff. But I often would get headaches from flying or was really tired in mid-flight - well that time, I was actually able to plan a lot of things while on the plane, writing them down and thinking straight and clear - was amazing and the 2 hours felt like 5 minutes. Drinking extra water before and after travel is also good....and doing CT at destination before and after you leave.

Also I took a lot of extra Ubiquinol which was great, I think. Perhaps you can exchange back rubs, even with clothes on, and try to get real air if you are staying over (ie. going outside in layover cities) if there is time.

I also take extra iodine to help with the bad air and germs etc.

And I love using some lavender essential oil to help with relaxing during flight and after...it's amazing. I had the best sleep after a recent trip though I didn't sleep a second on the flight, and did watch some movies when not chatting with my interesting neighbors from Egypt.

I think in general, when you do the various Jack Kruse protocols anyway, before and after your trip bc it's a lifestyle, it helps a lot.

Congrats to finding this place and transforming your health!! I have ONLY been following all this since last fall and was amazed at the difference it's made in how I felt with the jet lag coming and going too. So great.

I have never fasted before but I do bring my own food now (smoked salmon, canned sardines, smoked mackerel have worked great, and then some nuts, and for veggies, I have taken sauerkraut still warm in a small food thermos, or any other veggie like broccoli or cauliflower). I may pick at their vegetables if there is a meal but that's it. I happily (though maybe I shouldn't be happy) share the goodies they give out with neighbors. And I only drink the water they have when it comes out the bottle that I can see them pour. Otherwise it's tap and awfully chlorinated and whatever else. The beauty of having done the Leptin Rx and still not ever snacking has made traveling so much easier for me food wise. I don't feel like I have to worry about maybe getting hungry. My body is now so used to the three meals a day (which I still do, maybe some day down to two?) and I don't get hungry in between, it's very freeing....when I used to be making sure I had a snack just in case I might get hungry so I wouldn't need airport or plane food.

IF you can, I'd also suggest a magnetico sleep pad (for when you are at home). It's the most amazing thing I have invested in and totally worth it. It will help you tremendously coming back....at least I believe it helped me. IN fact, I now so strongly believe in it, I consider it more important than any supplement. At least if you live in a high EMF area, as I do. So on a recent transcontinental trip with 6 hours time difference, I came back, and slept around 8 hours each night, through the night, from about 9 to 10pm at night to 5-6:30am in the morning. Never experienced such greatness before. I was still tired during the day a few days, but no weird sleeping stuff. Though of course adjusting from a Westward flight always seems easier...

As to the Schumann generator I have no idea, I don't own one, yet.

I too am looking forward to hearing what others on this amazing board have to share.

Good luck and happy travels


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Increasing Insulin Sensitivity with melotonin (in rats)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increasing-insulin-sensitivity-with-melotonin-in-rats.7403/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dextery — 2013-06-20T02:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/increasing-insulin-sensitivity-with-melotonin-in-rats.7403/post-88457>

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jpi.12056/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jpi.12056/abstract)

Since I am an aged old rat with FBG between 105 & 120 I thought this study published in the **Journal of Pineal Research** was particularly apropos to my impaired insulin signaling pathway.

**Abstract**

In aged rats, insulin signaling pathway (ISP) is impaired in tissues that play a pivotal role in glucose homeostasis, such as liver, skeletal muscle, and adipose tissue. Moreover, the aging process is also associated with obesity and reduction in melatonin synthesis from the pineal gland and other organs. The aim of the present work was to evaluate, in male old obese Wistar rats, the effect of melatonin supplementation in the ISP, analyzing the total protein amount and the phosphorylated status (immunoprecipitation and immunoblotting) of the insulin cascade components in the rat hypothalamus, liver, skeletal muscle, and periepididymal adipose tissue. Melatonin was administered in the drinking water for 8- and 12 wk during the night period. Food and water intake and fasting blood glucose remained unchanged. The insulin sensitivity presented a 2.1-fold increase both after 8- and 12 wk of melatonin supplementation. Animals supplemented with melatonin for 12 wk also presented a reduction in body mass. The acute insulin-induced phosphorylation of the analyzed ISP proteins increased 1.3- and 2.3-fold after 8- and 12 wk of melatonin supplementation. The total protein content of the insulin receptor (IR) and the IR substrates (IRS-1, 2) remained unchanged in all investigated tissues, except for the 2-fold increase in the total amount of IRS-1 in the periepididymal adipose tissue. Therefore, the known age-related melatonin synthesis reduction may also be involved in the development of insulin resistance and the adequate supplementation could be an important alternative for the prevention of insulin signaling impairment in aged organisms
