> [http://jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/](http://jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/) read this oldie to gain weight

Thanks Jack. I've read that so many times and follow it already. My problems are in the head and actually acting on things. I do enjoy the raw cheeses though  I had forgotten about the heavy cream, its been a while since I've had that.
Previously you also said that I should not fear protein. That's one of the things I've really been working with. I'm trying to eat more protein and I definitely feel better doing so. Given I'm Danish I'm a huge fan of herring and its so cheap to get here too.

### Hemming — 2015-03-21T17:05:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159109>

Another great day in the gym. Not because I killed myself but because I was able to lift some heavy weights and it just felt good.

The challenge for me now is to keep momentum. I feel like I'm eating so much yet it looks like I'm getting leaner. I really enjoy all of the seafood and could eat so much fish - is there any limit to how much seafood I should be eating?

### Inger — 2015-03-21T20:53:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159133>

I am sure no limit, Hemming. I just eat as much as I can fit in
your brain screams for that stuff... as do mine...........
I want my brain to heal... you too?
so I give it loads of fat and DHA
and sunshine and darkness

### Hemming — 2015-03-21T21:17:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159140>

I sure do  I just feel like I eat so much food that I must be overeating instead of focusing on what my body needs. Its probably just because I'm used to not eating as much that I need to rewire my brain and not feel guilty for eating all of the good DHA and protein that come with seafood.

### Hemming — 2015-03-22T18:57:41+0200

### caroline — 2015-03-23T00:00:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159234>

such a great idea hemming ^^^^^

### Hemming — 2015-03-23T21:23:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159304>

I've scheduled an appointment for Wednesday. Can't wait to see what happens.

I was on the scale today for the first time in more than a month. A guy had poked for a couple of days to do it and I wanted to show that I can do it when I want to, not giving the scale any power. I was quite surprised to see that it was unchanged at best. I feel better and stronger than for a couple of years so its not because I'm relapsing.

### nicld — 2015-03-23T21:27:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159308>

That is HUGE, getting on the scale. Getting better.

### Hemming — 2015-03-23T22:10:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159311>

Yeah. It was more that it was like when most people get on the scale. You see a number and move on. I feel a lot less stigma about it now, it was a very simple, but for me profound, experience.

### Hemming — 2015-03-23T22:16:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159312>

And I've really learned the importance of getting good salt. As soon as I get enough my performance feels so good.

### caroline — 2015-03-24T01:37:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159317>

You are making great progress! proud of you!

Keep posting updates - it helps others.....wether positive or negative - it is a journey.

### nicld — 2015-03-24T21:21:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159372>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=159311)
>
> Yeah. It was more that it was like when most people get on the scale. You see a number and move on. I feel a lot less stigma about it now, it was a very simple, but for me profound, experience.

I just cannot do it yet. I even avoid them at the doctors office. Maybe next year.

### Hemming — 2015-03-26T09:57:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159455>

I don't want to step on the scale multiple times per month but I know feel that I have control of how the scale affects me and its not the scale telling me how to feel.

I was at the hypnotherapist yesterday. What an experience. We went back and talked about the time when I was home alone with my dad and he collapsed, that experience has 'haunted' me ever since. I went back with my present self and told 10y old self that I did everything I could at that point. I feel so relieved of that burden and I'm able to see myself differently, even physically I think I look diferent. We also talked about eating with feeling guilty etc.

### kovita — 2015-03-26T12:57:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159459>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=159455)
>
> I don't want to step on the scale multiple times per month but I know feel that I have control of how the scale affects me and its not the scale telling me how to feel.
>
> I was at the hypnotherapist yesterday. What an experience. We went back and talked about the time when I was home alone with my dad and he collapsed, that experience has 'haunted' me ever since. I went back with my present self and told 10y old self that I did everything I could at that point. I feel so relieved of that burden and I'm able to see myself differently, even physically I think I look diferent. We also talked about eating with feeling guilty etc.

that is great! i was myslef evaluating brain conditioning therapy such as hypnotherapy some time ago. It is pretty scary to realize how much past events and memories do interfere with our present life. Since i have children myself I do think often how many "wrong" memories I already seeded in their little brains. My girl aged 5 remembers events that happened when she was less than one year old in stunning details. I myself feel like there is a huge gap in my brain regarding childhood. I remember few things but there are whole years of my life i hardly recall any details. Maybe there is not a gap after all but rather an intention. I am finding out for myslef now i can handle my past ghost just by my own. It poped up as a "side effect" of my multilayer intention to get well that led me to completely different life and made me a different person in many ways.

### Hemming — 2015-03-26T21:58:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159497>

Children remember so many things. Even today I remember details of so many situations and when I told mom and dad they can't even remember the situation. I believe its very important to notice what your children take away from different situations. They might experience something you don't even notice. I don't think anyone thought about that it was a highly traumatising situation for me to watch my dad lifeless on the floor. I'm not blaming anyone for this, I would jsut wish that I had taken action myself sooner.

I honestly feel different and think thatt I look different. Its easier for me to eat, also in front of other people. In the next couple of days I'm going to try self hypnosis to see if I can get to the same state and work more with my memories in various situations.

### caroline — 2015-03-27T07:55:50+0200

So good Hemming .....great progress!

### notsoperfick — 2015-03-28T19:20:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159653>

I'm so pleased at your progress Hemming, you've moved on such a lot. Perhaps a way to address the little voice in your head is to carry something round with you, a little precis about how important it is not to look back? Something like these:[
One day at a time, this is enough. Don't look back and grieve over the past for it is gone. Do not be troubled about the future, for it has yet to come. Live in the present and make it so beautiful that it will be worth remembering.
\[URL='http://www.searchquotes.com/quotation/You%27ll\_never\_see\_the\_great\_things\_ahead\_of\_you\_if\_you\_keep\_looking\_at\_the\_bad\_things\_behind\_you.\_You/558891/'\]You'll never see the great things ahead of you if you keep looking at the bad things behind you. You are exactly where you need to be to reach your goals. Everything you've been through was preparation for where you are right now and where you can be tomorrow.](http://www.searchquotes.com/quotation/One_day_at_a_time%2C_this_is_enough._Don%27t_look_back_and_grieve_over_the_past_for_it_is_gone._Do_not_b/662344/) \[/URL\]
Don't look back, you're not going that way.

Write them on a piece of card and when you doubt or the voice starts speaking, top up your determination with a quick read to remind yourself that yesterday is not how you live any more.

### Hemming — 2015-03-30T22:08:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-back-to-a-normal-weight-from-the-other-end-of-the-scale.7553/post-159864>

> [One day at a time, this is enough. Don't look back and grieve over the past for it is gone. Do not be troubled about the future, for it has yet to come. Live in the present and make it so beautiful that it will be worth remembering.](http://www.searchquotes.com/quotation/One_day_at_a_time%2C_this_is_enough._Don%27t_look_back_and_grieve_over_the_past_for_it_is_gone._Do_not_b/662344/)
> [You'll never see the great things ahead of you if you keep looking at the bad things behind you. You are exactly where you need to be to reach your goals. Everything you've been through was preparation for where you are right now and where you can be tomorrow.](http://www.searchquotes.com/quotation/You%27ll_never_see_the_great_things_ahead_of_you_if_you_keep_looking_at_the_bad_things_behind_you._You/558891/)
> Don't look back, you're not going that way.
>
> Write them on a piece of card and when you doubt or the voice starts speaking, top up your determination with a quick read to remind yourself that yesterday is not how you live any more.

Very nice words and thanks for the support! I've previously done something like that but I really like the way you phrase it

I feel so much better overall. I feel free of the guilt and shame. I feel that I can do what I want, not what I think everyone is expecting of me. I feel like I've found myself again. I know there will be bumbs on the road but there's no reason to be concerned about that now. Now I just want to move on.

# Thread: F.Lux .... do you also need the glasses?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-do-you-also-need-the-glasses.7555/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MeghanK — 2013-07-05T04:31:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-do-you-also-need-the-glasses.7555/post-89510>

If you have F.LUX on your computer... do you need the gunnar or orange glasses too?

### Melinda — 2013-07-05T06:04:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-do-you-also-need-the-glasses.7555/post-89511>

You do when you get up to get more structured water from the refrigerator and the light blinds you! We unscrewed some of the refrig lights but it is surprising how many times I get zapped by forgetting that I need very low light as I move around the house. Over time I have gotten rid of the problems but it only takes one exposure. I put the glasses on about an hour before I want to go to sleep as they stupefy me in about 60 minutes and I don't have to worry about an unwanted exposure.

### Danco3636 — 2013-07-05T09:00:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-do-you-also-need-the-glasses.7555/post-89512>

I were mine. I also wear my Gunnar's anytime I'm on the computer.

### caroline — 2013-07-05T11:45:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-do-you-also-need-the-glasses.7555/post-89516>

Mine don't stupify me anymore - I used to be out like a light in 60 minutes too ..... not any more ...wonder why?

### MeghanK — 2013-07-05T17:06:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-do-you-also-need-the-glasses.7555/post-89535>

So even your regular 75 watt or whatever bulbs in your kitchen living room bedroom etc... effect you too without the glasses?

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-05T19:44:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-do-you-also-need-the-glasses.7555/post-89541>

I wear my glasses as soon as it starts to get dark.. in the winter time I' wear them as soon as I get home. I also wear them during the day when I'm at work b/c I work in a windowless office building and the only lighting is florescent bulbs and I get migraines by the end of the day if I don't wear them...

### Melinda — 2013-07-05T20:44:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-do-you-also-need-the-glasses.7555/post-89548>

> [MeghanK said:](/goto/post?id=89535)
>
> So even your regular 75 watt or whatever bulbs in your kitchen living room bedroom etc... effect you too without the glasses?

Yes, they did so I changed all my lamps to 25w and I still put the glasses on when it gets dark or about an hour before I want to fall asleep.

### caroline — 2013-07-06T05:50:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-do-you-also-need-the-glasses.7555/post-89578>

Yep - I have no lights on when the sun sets........

# Thread: Pregnenolone experiences
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnenolone-experiences.7560/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2013-07-05T20:32:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnenolone-experiences.7560/post-89545>

Hi guys,

I have recently been taking pregnenolone (100mg) for about 2 weeks now. I have been doing this as I believe I have adrenal fatigue (no tests/can't afford) and my digestion is a constant, chronic source of stress/inflammation. I hoped it would battle the stress and help my adrenals.

I have noticed an improvement in mental clarity, skin quality, hair/excema, and a slight improvment in libido. However I have also had more heart palpatations (worse than usual), and some acne.

What are your/dr. Ks opinions on this. I think I read somewhere that if your T3 is bad (dunno if mine is) then you shouldn't take preg.

I would like to know what causes this acne and will it go away after a while, is it a kind of detox symptom?

Also has anyone else taken preg? What were your experiences?

Thanks, Sean.

### JanSz — 2013-07-05T20:56:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnenolone-experiences.7560/post-89550>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=89545)
>
> Hi guys,
>
> I have recently been taking pregnenolone (100mg) for about 2 weeks now. I have been doing this as I believe I have adrenal fatigue (no tests/can't afford) and my digestion is a constant, chronic source of stress/inflammation. I hoped it would battle the stress and help my adrenals.
>
> I have noticed an improvement in mental clarity, skin quality, hair/excema, and a slight improvment in libido. However I have also had more heart palpatations (worse than usual), and some acne.
>
> What are your/dr. Ks opinions on this. I think I read somewhere that if your T3 is bad (dunno if mine is) then you shouldn't take preg.
>
> I would like to know what causes this acne and will it go away after a while, is it a kind of detox symptom?
>
> Also has anyone else taken preg? What were your experiences?
>
> Thanks, Sean.

Sean
is that male or female?

I am a man.
I use supplemental pregnenolone.
My experience is highly positive.

I have tried numerous pregnenolone products, pills and transdermal delivery, always followed up by blood tests (and self evaluation).

The only pregnenolone that works for me is

Micronized Lipid Matrix from Nutricology, 150mg tabs

Starting, I had hardly detectable levels of pregnenolone, progesterone and low Cortisol.
At first, titrating dose and following with blood tests, I ended up with 900mg/day dose.
After about a year on that dose, I must have apparently filled up enough deficiencies,
because preg, prog & cortisol levels stated to raise excessively.

Titrating down I ended on 300mg/day and am currently on that dose for over a year.

It is important to also supplement with DHEA.
Low DHEAs is so common that lef.org recommends supplementation first and follow up checkup latter.

I am currently using 100mg/day DHEA

DHEA Micronized Lipid Matrix from Allergy Research
I am currentlt trying this product.

Previously I was using 400mg/day DHEA from lef.org

\--------------------------------------------------------------------

Using pregnenolone (the type that actually works)
should improve Cortisol.

Better cortisol should allow to process more T3.

What follows depends on T4 & T3 stores and ability to convert T4---->T3

If one is using supplemental thyroid hormones
but
have low cortisol
he will not be able to process it.

Raising cortisol may allow more T3 to be processed, sometime excessively, hence heart palpitations.

If you have not supplemented with thyroid hormones prior to taking pregnenolone,
those palpitations are expected to be short lasting.

Preferred way is to establish good cortisol level first
then
follow up with thyroid hormone supplementation if required.

\-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Libido depends on estrogen & progesterone status.
Raising pregnenolone, you have improved on progesterone, that may have helped.

Progesterone and estrogens need to be in balance.
Preferably with excess of progesterone.

Low progesterone creates excess estrogens regardless of actual estrogens levels.

Excess estrogens promotes tissue growth.
Men may/will grow breasts.

Common in men with prostate cancer who are on hormone ablation therapy.

///

### seanb4 — 2013-07-06T14:20:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnenolone-experiences.7560/post-89590>

Awesome that it has worked for you JanSz.

In answer to your question I am male, 22yo.

So you are saying that the heart palpitations mean the preg is increasing my T3 and this is a good thing?

Also is the acne from die off, hormones adjusting or is it simply too much pregnenolone? What I am asking is, is it a good sign (body changing for the better) or a bad sign (overdosing on certain hormones)?

Thanks for your detailed response btw.

### seanb4 — 2013-07-06T14:21:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnenolone-experiences.7560/post-89591>

also I am not taking DHEA but my understanding is that if my body needs it, it can produce it from the pregnenolone. Is this true?

### Hope — 2013-07-08T01:46:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnenolone-experiences.7560/post-89683>

Seanb4, have you read Jack's post on DHEA? THe one from 6-30-2012. Highly recommend you read it. You are you young so you could be OK but if you have issues, maybe not. I am a lot older than you and I can tell you adding DHEA - the real DHEA, not the 7-keto - seems to have made a huge difference in my healing. More than many other supplements, largely bc it helped sleep.

What is your sleep like these days? You wake up refreshed? Also where do you live? Ie big city?

Also, if you can at all, save up some money to get tested for three labs....Vitamin D, DHEA and HS CRP.

JanSz has said a lot of great stuff already, and the article he referenced is also great - have you read it? He's got a ton of knowledge...and is so kind to share so much on here

### seanb4 — 2013-07-13T11:55:46+0300

### seanb4 — 2013-07-18T18:22:00+0300

### seanb4 — 2013-08-08T20:10:32+0300

### WereBear — 2014-07-16T21:10:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pregnenolone-experiences.7560/post-134602>

I know this is an old thread but it's the first one I've run across with pregnenolone. With menopause I had insomnia, memory issues, and homicidal mood-swings - given PremPro - which stopped those issues but I think I traded a respite for further problems like my Adrenal Fatigue. However, I stopped the HRT and switched to 50 mg of pregnenolone a day, under the tongue, with fantastic results for 6 months now.

Most of what I read of people taking preg and running into trouble were people who reported already being on some kind of hormonal supplement... so it's important that you don't overload, it seems.

### JanSz — 2014-07-18T17:47:42+0300

More than many other supplements, largely bc it helped sleep.
>
> What is your sleep like these days? You wake up refreshed? Also where do you live? Ie big city?
>

>

There are three types of DHEA that come often in discussions.
DHEA
DHEAs
7keto-DHEA

I measure DHEA and DHEAs in serum.
I do not know how to measure 7keto-DHEA. (Someone pls help)

7keto-DHEA have thermogenic effects on the body, may help in adjusting thyroid hormones, burns body fat. Doses 100-200mg/day.

In my own experience, I can regulate my DHEAs using DHEA pills in rather reliable way, but I need to use large doses, sometimes within 300-400mg/day.

Unfortunately my DHEA,serum levels remain low.
I could use some suggestions on how to improve on that.

# Thread: CT with calorie restriction.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-with-calorie-restriction.7561/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2013-07-05T20:40:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-with-calorie-restriction.7561/post-89546>

Hi guys,

I think I have chronic inflammation/stress from my digestive system every time I eat or drink anything (even water). This causes bloating, fatigue, etc.
The only thing that seems to work for this is reducing the volume of stuff I put in my digestive system. This means I eat lower calories to manage my symptoms.

I have read that doing CT reduces the need for calories due to improved celular efficiency (I think) but I have also read that it burns more calories through heat.

So my question would be, is it beneficial to do CT with calorie restriction? Or would it make me worse because of increasing need of calories and thus more volume of food in my digestive system?

Thanks guys, Sean.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-05T22:27:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-with-calorie-restriction.7561/post-89554>

Read CT 4......it talks about it.

### seanb4 — 2013-07-06T16:05:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-with-calorie-restriction.7561/post-89599>

"our metabolism become super efficient in cold allowing you to Eat LESS calories while maintaining unreal performance". Is this what your talking about?

So cold can burn lots of calories if your body has excess fat but if it doesn't have excess fat cold will allow you to eat less whilst maintaining fat stores?

### MeghanK — 2013-07-06T17:27:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-with-calorie-restriction.7561/post-89603>

Good Question seanb4... I was wondering something similar... if you need to gain weight and are doing CT do you need to eat more?

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-07-06T20:49:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-with-calorie-restriction.7561/post-89610>

^I was wondering this too since I'm trying to gain weight. I just kind of calculated my maintenance calories through a method of Lyle McDonald's, but I haven't even really been gaining and I was wondering both how many calories a simple ten minute cold shower could burn, and how much it raises my metabolism, thus burning even more calories. I've also been worried about the possible mismatches I could be creating myself, but I'm not trying to change the topic from what seanb4 originally created.

### MeghanK — 2013-07-07T18:46:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-with-calorie-restriction.7561/post-89662>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-with-calorie-restriction.7561/post-89664>

> [MeghanK said:](/goto/post?id=89662)
>
> Hmmmm. has anyone every tried to quantify calorie burn or after calorie burn from CT?

Yes, that is exactly what Ray Cronise is doing at [http://hypothermics.com/category/blog/](http://hypothermics.com/category/blog/) . His findings are very interesting and show that CT is very effective at burning calories. His diet recommendations are almost completely opposite of Jack's (just fyi).

I would be really interesting to hear Jack's response to seanb4 question though.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-07T19:38:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-with-calorie-restriction.7561/post-89665>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=89599)
>
> "our metabolism become super efficient in cold allowing you to Eat LESS calories while maintaining unreal performance". Is this what your talking about?
>
> So cold can burn lots of calories if your body has excess fat but if it doesn't have excess fat cold will allow you to eat less whilst maintaining fat stores?

Cold always causes you to burn fat as calories..........the colder it gets the more sensitive your steroid receptors get to lower amount of free steroids in your plasma. It allows you to compensate for the lowered amount of steroids from CR that occurs during winter months. If you do not have fat you wont be able to calorie burn..........rarely does this happen in nature in wild animals because they lose their summer and autumn fat in winter.......if they do not get their fat stores high enough they do not make it through winter. Skinny humans make it by covering themselves to protect from cold and this reduces their free T3 further.

So that I often feel cold, to varying degrees, is actually because of my low weight/body fat and that I'm not eating enough? I haven't had my T3 tested but my TSH is 1.34.

Also, by being cold your body can operate better with low circulating hormones, which are caused by calorie restriction, and thus is able to maintain your weight better with lower calories?

Thanks Jack.

# Thread: MrPinkies lab results.. Thoughts?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mrpinkies-lab-results-thoughts.7562/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MrPinkies — 2013-07-05T20:41:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mrpinkies-lab-results-thoughts.7562/post-89547>

Ok friends..

So.. They decided to run the tests that they wanted.. and my appointment is at the end of the month when we can "discuss" the other labs that I wanted and go over these results.. PLEASE if you think of something that I should have tested, let me know! I know the HSCRP and the reverse T3's are on my list already.. If anyone is curious, I am 125lbs lighter from 2010.. Even though in the last year I have lost a LOT of weight, I still feel that something isn't right just yet...

Here we go!

2013:

COMP Metabolic Panel:
ALB: 4.1 Albumin Healthy range: 3.9 to 5.0 g/dL
AP: 59 I'm not sure what this is.. but range is 27-120 Alkaline phosphatase?
BILIT: 0.7 Bilirubin Healthy range: 0.1 to 1.9 mg/dL
BUN: 11 BUN (blood urea nitrogen) Healthy range: 10 to 20 mg/dL
CAL: 9.3 Calcium Healthy range: 9.0 to 10.5 mg/dL
CD: 23 I'm not sure what this is.. but range is 20-30
CHL: 106 Chloride Healthy range: 98 to 106 mEq/L
CREAT: 0.74 Creatinine Healthy range: 0.5 to 1.1 mg/dL
GFRBF: 111 I'm not sure what this is.. but range is >60
GFROF: 92 I'm not sure what this is.. but range is >60
GLU: 99 Glucose? range 60-110
GOT: 18 I'm not sure what this is... but range is 15-41
GPT: 19 I'm not sure what this is... but range is 10-54
POT: 4.0 Potassium Healthy range: 3.7 to 5.2 mEq/L
PRO: 6.8 I'm not sure what this is... but range is 6.1-7.9 Protein?
SOD: 137 Sodium Healthy range: 135 to 145 mEq/L

Lipid profile:
CHD RISK: 2.8 I'm guessing that this is Total cholesterol/HDL ratio range <4.9
CHOL: 179 cholesterol <200 mg/dL (below 5.18 mmol/L)
HDL: 64 HDL cholesterol >40 mg/dL
LDL: 103 LDL cholesterol <129 mg/dL
TRIG: 60 Triglycerides Healthy range: 40 to 160 mg/dL

Complete Blood Count:
HCT: 42.3% Hematocrit 36.0-48.0%
HGB: 13.9 Hemoglobin 12 to 16 g/dL
MCHC: 32.8% Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) Normal range: 28% to 36%
MCV: 91 Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) Normal range: 80 to 100 femtoliters
PLT: 248,000 I'm guessing that this is Platelet count Normal range: 150,000 to 400,000 mL
RBC: 4.63 RBC (red blood cell) erythrocyte count Normal range: 4.2 to 5.9 million cmm
RDW: 14.7% Red cell distribution width (RDW or RCDW) Normal range: 11% to 15%
WBC: 7,600 WBC (white blood cell) leukocyte count Normal range: 4,300 to 10,800 cmm

To kinda compare from my last labs back in 2010:
TSH: 1.65 Thyroid-stimulating hormone Healthy range is 0.3 to 3
BUN: 12
CAL: 9.2
CD: 26
CHL: 101
CREAT: 0.60
GFRBF: 144
GFROF: 119
GLU: 88
POT: 3.7
SOD: 139
CHD RISK:3.3
CHOL: 197
HDL: 60
LDL: 118
TRIG: 96

> GPT: 19 I'm not sure what this is... but range is 10-54
> POT: 4.0 Potassium Healthy range: 3.7 to 5.2 mEq/L
> PRO: 6.8 I'm not sure what this is... but range is 6.1-7.9
> SOD: 137 Sodium Healthy range: 135 to 145 mEq/L
>
> Lipid profile:
> CHD RISK: 2.8 I'm guessing that this is Total cholesterol/HDL ratio range <4.9
> CHOL: 179 cholesterol <200 mg/dL (below 5.18 mmol/L)
> HDL: 64 HDL cholesterol >40 mg/dL
> LDL: 103 LDL cholesterol <129 mg/dL
> TRIG: 60 Triglycerides Healthy range: 40 to 160 mg/dL
>
> Complete Blood Count:
> HCT: 42.3% Hematocrit 36.0-48.0%
> HGB: 13.9 Hemoglobin 12 to 16 g/dL
> MCHC: 32.8% Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) Normal range: 28% to 36%
> MCV: 91 Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) Normal range: 80 to 100 femtoliters
> PLT: 248,000 I'm guessing that this is Platelet count Normal range: 150,000 to 400,000 mL
> RBC: 4.63 RBC (red blood cell) erythrocyte count Normal range: 4.2 to 5.9 million cmm
> RDW: 14.7% Red cell distribution width (RDW or RCDW) Normal range: 11% to 15%
> WBC: 7,600 WBC (white blood cell) leukocyte count Normal range: 4,300 to 10,800 cmm
>
>
> To kinda compare from my last labs back in 2010:
> TSH: 1.65 Thyroid-stimulating hormone Healthy range is 0.3 to 3
> BUN: 12
> CAL: 9.2
> CD: 26
> CHL: 101
> CREAT: 0.60
> GFRBF: 144
> GFROF: 119
> GLU: 88
> POT: 3.7
> SOD: 139
> CHD RISK:3.3
> CHOL: 197
> HDL: 60
> LDL: 118
> TRIG: 96

Kate: Good changes Triglycerides down to 60 from 96. The lower the better.
HDL needs to go up to 70 or above. The higher the better. You can raise HDL by supplementing with niacin. Slo-Niacin is the product I use with minimal flushing. I take 500mg am and 500mg pm.

Bun/Creat ratio was 14 and now is 20 indicating you are hydrating well.

Suggested tests to run: NMR Lipid profile will give you the breakdown of your LDL total particles and then it will tell you if the majority of the particles are large bouyant that do no harm to the vascular system.

On the Thyroid side, TSH, T3, Free T3, T4, Reverse T3, high sensitivity Crp, Vit D3 serum level
Hopefully others will have more insight after the July 4 weekend.

### MrPinkies — 2013-07-07T19:46:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mrpinkies-lab-results-thoughts.7562/post-89666>

Thank for the eye opening insight, Dexter! I'm really happy about my triglycerides and cholesterol too.. I am curious why they didn't run the TSH like they did in 2010.. I will definitely ask for those tests.. I just feel like something is off..

### HoneyChild — 2013-07-08T12:57:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mrpinkies-lab-results-thoughts.7562/post-89694>

I've been reading up on magnesium lately and it looks as though most of us are deficient due to poor quality soils. Over the years the ref rage for RBC magnesium has been going down and down as is mentioned in the following link:

[http://gotmag.org/how-to-restore-magnesium/](http://gotmag.org/how-to-restore-magnesium/)

Look at the mag deficiency symptoms to see if any of them are familiar to you [http://gotmag.org/magnesium-deficiency-101/](http://gotmag.org/magnesium-deficiency-101/)

If you think this is relevant to you then I suggest you join the FB group: [https://www.facebook.com/groups/MagnesiumAdvocacy/](https://www.facebook.com/groups/MagnesiumAdvocacy/)

Good luck

### MrPinkies — 2013-07-08T20:34:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mrpinkies-lab-results-thoughts.7562/post-89711>

# Thread: Migraines:  a deficiency in current of DHA/CSF semiconductors in brain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraines-a-deficiency-in-current-of-dha-csf-semiconductors-in-brain.7564/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-05T22:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraines-a-deficiency-in-current-of-dha-csf-semiconductors-in-brain.7564/post-89556>

[http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/807274?nlid=31945\_1049&src=wnl\_edit\_dail&uac=180207PN](http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/807274?nlid=31945_1049&src=wnl_edit_dail&uac=180207PN)

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-06T15:40:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraines-a-deficiency-in-current-of-dha-csf-semiconductors-in-brain.7564/post-89595>

Link doesn't work....

### Dextery — 2013-07-06T18:00:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraines-a-deficiency-in-current-of-dha-csf-semiconductors-in-brain.7564/post-89605>

**Migraines: a deficiency in current of DHA/CSF semiconductors in brain**

Anything at medscape.com requires a free username and password.

Here is the article.

BOSTON, Massachusetts â€” Positron emission tomography of patients experiencing the premonitory phase of migraine, prior to the headache setting in, shows activation in several areas of the brain, indicating that migraine is a brain disorder and not a response to pain stimuli.

The results are significant in terms of understanding the neurobiology of migraine and could have future implications for drug treatment, said study author Peter James Goadsby, MD, PhD, professor, neurology, and director, Headache Program, University of California at San Francisco, and president, International Headache Society.

"This is an important step in solidifying our ideas that migraine is fundamentally a disorder of the brain, not a disorder of structures outside the brain," said Dr. Goadsby. "We were able to address the question that people have wondered about for many, many years, that is, what is the degree to which pain is driving the initial symptomatology â€” and we got clear answers to that."

Dr. Goadsby and his colleagues won the Harold G. Wolff Lecture Award for this research during the 2013 International Headache Congress (IHC).

Subtle Symptoms

Premonitory symptoms of migraine can include yawning, neck discomfort, nausea, thirst, photophobia, phonophobia, craving sweet or savory foods, and mood swings. It's not clear what proportion of patients with migraine experience these early symptoms, which are often quite subtle, Dr. Goadsby said. Estimates vary widely, from about a third to 80%.

In the past, this symptomatology has not received much medical attention, said Dr. Goadsby. Physicians might not ask about premonitory symptoms because this information doesn't influence their diagnosis.

In years gone by, people used to think of migraine as a disorder of the blood vessels. In more recent times, the view has been that migraine is a reaction to pain stimuli. "I think our new research suggests that this is just not true," said Dr. Goadsby.

Using nitroglycerin, a well-documented trigger for migraine, researchers induced premonitory symptoms in patients who have migraine without aura. Instead of waiting for headache onset to begin scanning the patients' brains, as has been done in the past, researchers did the scanning during the premonitory phase. Eight patients had at least 1 premonitory scan without pain.

"Before this, all the imaging of migraine has been during the headache and the question has risen as to the degree to which what's happening in the brain is just a response to pain, or is something more fundamental, a part of the process of the migraine," said Dr. Goadsby. "By studying the premonitory symptoms, you get rid of that question because these patients don't have any pain."

Neuronal Activation

Researchers used H2 15O (radioactive water) to measure regional cerebral blood flow as a surrogate marker for neuronal activation.

They found that compared with baseline scans, there was activation in several key areas, including the hypothalamus, an area involved in low-level regulation of sleep, appetite, mood, and fluids. "It seems likely that the hypothalamus is pivotal in the onset of migraine," commented Dr. Goadsby.

Other structures that were activated included the midbrain, around the periaqueductal grey, which has been shown to be active during a migraine attack, and an area in the pons that past migraine imaging has also shown to be active.

"This shows you the areas of the brain that are involved at the earliest in the attack," said Dr. Goadsby.

Scans of the 8 patients plus another 2 patients experiencing photophobia symptoms, again before they felt any pain, showed activation in the visual cortex. "This suggests that the photophobia experience can be dissected away from the pain experience," said Dr. Goadsby.

Similarly, scans of patients experiencing nausea had activation of an area of the medulla that includes nausea and vomiting centers. "So it's entirely plausible that those areas are activated by the migraine process and that's why nausea and vomiting are so common in migraine; it's not simply a response to the pain," said Dr. Goadsby.

"It was thought that nausea and pain were highly linked, but that doesn't seem to necessarily be the case," he added.

Dr. Goadsby hopes the research will "shift thinking" to consider migraine as a brain disorder, but he stressed that this should not lessen the importance of the pain that migraine patients suffer.

From a big picture treatment perspective, this says to me that we probably won't get away with developing drugs that don't get into the brain to have substantial effects on migraine prevention. Dr. Peter James Goadsby

The research could have ramifications for treatment in that the most obvious target would be the brain, but developing targeted therapies that don't have adverse effects could be challenging.

"From a big picture treatment perspective, this says to me that we probably won't get away with developing drugs that don't get into the brain to have substantial effects on migraine prevention," said Dr. Goadsby.

He noted that to date, the best proven migraine prevention therapies are anticonvulsant drugs, tricyclic antidepressants, and the β-blocker propranolol, all of which affect the brain. This, he said, is consistent with the theory that migraine is a disorder of the brain.

Relevant to Clinicians

Asked to comment on the study, Elizabeth E. Loder, MD, president, American Headache Society, associate professor, neurology, Harvard Medical School, and head, Division of Headache and Pain, Brigham and Women's Hospital, Boston, said it's nice to have a biological explanation for what patients experience and that the research might eventually lead to treatment.

"I was particularly heartened by the fact that this year's winner \[of the Wolff Lecture Award\] was able to say so many things that were relevant to clinicians," said Dr. Loder. "We all see patients who describe knowing through various signs and symptoms that they are likely to get a migraine attack and it's good to hear what the biological underpinnings for that recognition of an impending attack might be."

Dr. Loder said she and her colleagues wonder how this new information might be exploited to prevent attacks, not only with drugs but also behaviorally.

She doesn't think clinicians ignore the premonitory phase of migraine so much as lack the evidence to support making recommendations about what to do in these circumstances. "Most patientsâ€¦already know what to do; for example, if they're hungry and feel an attack coming that eating may help or to putting sunglasses on in a bight sunny place."

Dr. Goadsby has received consulting fees/honoraria from Allergen, CoLucid, MSP, MSD, eNeura, ATI, Boston Sci, Eli Lilly, Medtronic, BMS, Pfizer, Nevro Corp, Impax, Zogenix, and Dr. Reddy's and research grants from MAP, MSD, Amgen, and Electrocore.

# Thread: Too high DHEA?

### Hope — 2013-07-05T23:35:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-high-dhea.7565/post-89557>

So I have been doing DHEA and Pregnenolone since April and it appears my DHEA is now above the top of the range.....as in 463 and the top is 325 in my range. My pregnenolone has nearly tripled from the mid thirties to 95,top is 188 so I have a ways to go.

Other levels as I remember are OK too, like D is in the mid 90s, HS CRP is still low at 0.2. Thyroid still or again at low normal range for T3 but no anitbods and no RT3 issues and I feel much better......I will share more details/labs later but am on the road and couldn't wait to ask on here....has anyone overshot their DHEA? Is that OK for some time? I sleep and feel like a rockstar - at least when at home (I struggled on vacation for some reason) when I have full darkness and my magnetico. I believe DHEA has something to do with it as well.

Would love to hear from anyone and will also reread the H 101 blog too....

# Thread: CT Newbie
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### beach bunny — 2013-07-06T06:00:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89579>

I am hoping this really counts towards CTing because I am so excited about it...

I went camping 2 nights this week. Both mornings I had a huge BAB and did not get hungry until dinner!
The lake was still but really freezing both days.
First day was hot but not sunny so I barely made myself go in to the water to my knees.
Second day was extremely hot and sunny (35deg C) and I was able to get in and out of the lake up until my neck multiple times!

Although I think I got sun stroke from too much sun too quickly (also burned a bit), the combination of the hot sun (photoelectric) and cold lake water, as well as sleeping on the ground, breathing fresh air, no EMFs has left me feeling more energized than I have all YEAR!

Is this way of doing CT still beneficial?

### caroline — 2013-07-06T06:22:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89582>

YES, YES, YES ...... that just sounds perfect! The more cold adapted you are and the more C/T - it seems we don't burn and get a lot darker. I had the most incredible sun tan this past summer and didn't burn once - even when in the sun for hours. In retrospect maybe that wasn't so smart - but I never did burn..... and I still am tanned even tho I am freezing!

### beach bunny — 2013-07-06T15:24:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89594>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=89582)
>
> YES, YES, YES ...... that just sounds perfect! The more cold adapted you are and the more C/T - it seems we don't burn and get a lot darker. I had the most incredible sun tan this past summer and didn't burn once - even when in the sun for hours. In retrospect maybe that wasn't so smart - but I never did burn..... and I still am tanned even tho I am freezing!

Thank you so much Caroline!! I didn't realize one of the other CT perks was a better tan without burning haha! That is so awesome!

I am really far from cold adapted that is for sure, my thermostat is so out of whack! Even under that burning sun it took a while for my ice cold extremities to start to feel nice and warm. Throughout winter all my health issues prevented me from being able to shock my body with cold (except finishing with cold showers). So I have avoided the cold for as long as I can remember. I have always felt better in the warmer summer months/warmer climate until brain infections took over. Then I couldn't be in the sun either without feeling sick. But working on those too.

My top priority is to move somewhere coastal where I have access to ocean CT, sunshine and as much seafood as I can eat haha! In the meantime I'm still trying to do what I can to incorporate CT gradually into my life.

### caroline — 2013-07-07T11:16:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89640>

Good Luck Beach Bunny with moving to a coastal location .... I hope you can make it happen sooner rather than later. As JK has said ......we really only have this moment .... no time is promised to us....... so pack your bikini an get moving. I know a great beach....

Why do you call yourself beach bunny? Is there a story there? \[that you can share  \]

### Hemming — 2013-07-07T13:35:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89649>

FWIW, I've also noticed that I don't burn anymore and my tan does look nicer.

Something else I've noticed is that some of my "moles" (or whatever the right word is) that I wasn't born with have become lighter. I'm really curious to see if it continues and that they could almost go away.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-07-07T18:32:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89660>

That lake camping sounds wonderful! We did that, too a few weeks ago and I left feeling exhilarated. Three days of sleeping on the ground, being barefoot all day, lying in the sand under the sun, and super cold lake water is what I'd love to have access to all summer long. Since it's become hot where I live, my ability to do deep CT has decreased substantially. So I do what I can with what I have. Cold showers and baths. I add ice to my bathwater, as much as I can. I swim a lot with my kids so I air-dry off (usually in the wind, which always makes it feel colder!). I've noticed that this summer, I rarely need to apply sunscreen. Sometimes I just add a bit to my nose. This is the first summer I haven't had a single burn. I attribute that to all my cold spring soaking all winter long!

### beach bunny — 2013-07-08T02:27:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89686>

@ Caroline: Omg you know whatâ€™s hilariousâ€¦ I tell my bf all the time that if our EMF infested condo ever burned down (be careful what you wish for right?) the only thing I would save is my BEACH BAG which is in the closet right by the front door! Hahaha!
(My bf used to call me bunny all the timeâ€¦and when I went backpacking through South America it evolved into beach bunny because I was doing so much beach hopping hehe.)

@ Hemming: That is really exciting about your â€œmolesâ€. It seems like it does wonders for skin health!

@Chrysanthemama: I know donâ€™t you love it! I canâ€™t wait to go back in a few weeks and I also wish I had access to the perfect natural oasis all the time. Iâ€™m so excited that you guys have noticed such a difference in the no burning.

Even though Iâ€™m nervous I think I need to step it up and really try to get into my tub. I wonâ€™t make the water too cold to start off. I will try it in the next week and see how I do.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-07-08T02:43:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89687>

Having had my own house, and the houses of my in-laws, and my husband's grandmother, and his uncle burn down, I can say that it's a remarkably freeing experience. We lost all our homes in a wildfire that burned through our town, and we were right in the crosshairs of there worst part of the burn.

We took our dogs, our bikes, our martial arts uniforms. And yes, my swim bag was right by the door, so I took that too.

### beach bunny — 2013-07-08T03:08:46+0300

>

OMG Chrys! You just made all the hairs on my neck stand up. I cannot believe it... All of your homes gone just like that!
Is it awful that I want to be able to move so badly that I want this home to burn too? It's like what is wrong with me right?

### beach bunny — 2013-07-08T20:04:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89707>

So I just attempted to do face dunking on my balcony for the first time! I used RO water with RO ice added to it. I did not measure the temperature of the water with a thermometer but I did not make it too cold just to see how I would feel.

I dunked my head a total of 3x for about 45 seconds each time. I have nerve issues (from viral/infection damage) and I felt the immediate tingling in the ones in my uterus from the cold shock. Then I felt the nerve in my right sciatica start to tingle and the sensation went all the way down my right leg (where there is another injury).

Now it has been about 15 minutes since the CT and I am feeling very sleepy and drowsy.
Maybe it is too much for my brain infections too fast

Dr. K is this dangerous for me to be doing?

### caroline — 2013-07-09T10:37:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-newbie.7567/post-89749>

The one thing you could have done better Beach Bunny .....when you were camping - you should have gone fishing and then cooked your fresh fish on the Barbie ..... Now we are talking!

I just noticed that you are in Toronto ... I was born not far from sunny side!

# Thread: Desiccated Thyroid - Can You Share Your Experience?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/desiccated-thyroid-can-you-share-your-experience.7575/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-07-06T20:50:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/desiccated-thyroid-can-you-share-your-experience.7575/post-89611>

I talked to my primary Dr. briefly the other day and told her the situation with my thyroid (low T3 and High TSH).

She said she would probably want to put me on Synthroid but is open to Armour but we need to talk more - my appt is on Monday morning

I'm absolutely not going on Synthroid but will consider Armour or something similar.

I know there are several people here who do take a desiccated thyroid and was wondering if you could share your experience - good or bad.

thanks for your input

### Jonathin — 2013-07-07T00:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/desiccated-thyroid-can-you-share-your-experience.7575/post-89623>

Check your auto-immune status with thyroid antibodies tests.

Check reverse T3.

Keep checking TSH and T3 until the numbers get right or close to it.

If your doctor thinks, as most do, that Synthroid is a good treatment for lack of thyroid function I would be cautious. My doctor thought that getting TSH just below 4.5 was adequate (from 6.5) and I should see him again a year. I was self pay, ordered my own blood test and still got this kind of treatment.

For me, desiccated thyroid proved more effective at lowering TSH than straight T3 (cytomel) and I feel better. It has been a year of figuring this out along with a number of other issues. What works for me or anybody else though should not be a guide for what is going to work for you. Learn as much as you can as soon as you can.

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-07T03:39:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/desiccated-thyroid-can-you-share-your-experience.7575/post-89628>

Love my Armour - you couldn't pay me to touch synthroid. With Chaos having possible hypothyroidism - when we get to the pedatric endocrinologist - if she goes on meds it will be compounded Armour - or another NDT. If I won't use it, my kids won't use it..

### cantweight — 2013-07-07T06:17:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/desiccated-thyroid-can-you-share-your-experience.7575/post-89632>

I've been on 150mg of Armour for years....since about 2006. I was put on synthroid briefly by my GP, but holistic doc stopped it immediately. I have been happy with it, although still hopeful that I can get off it at some point.

2 of my kids also took very small amounts for a short period of time as well as cytomel and TG 100 supplement. Their thyroid function improved dramatically has has been stable for a few years.

"As Sandberg mentioned in her book, our research shows that people can change their own hormone levels and behaviors, by "faking it" â€” by "power posing," or adopting expansive, open nonverbal postures that are strongly associated with power and dominance across the animal kingdom (imagine standing with hands on hips and feet spread, like Wonder Woman). Or should I just be at my computer with no mat? Any other hacks?

**
The problem with this is that when you plug those mats in the US at least, into the wall, they plug-in to the grounding outlets so theyâ€™re grounded and in the US, about 70% of the power that goes into our homes and our buildings travels back to the substation, not through the wires but through the ground. And the earth in the US is literally electrified with power travelling back to the substation so youâ€™re essentially introducing extra electrical pollution and extra electro-magnetic frequencies into your bed or wherever you happen to be sleeping or standing on this grounding mat when you use grounding or earthing mats in the US.**

### marcus — 2013-07-09T07:06:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-mats-better-than-nothing.7583/post-89744>

> [MeghanK said:](/goto/post?id=89701)
>
> **
> The problem with this is that when you plug those mats in the US at least, into the wall, they plug-in to the grounding outlets so theyâ€™re grounded and in the US, about 70% of the power that goes into our homes and our buildings travels back to the substation, not through the wires but through the ground. And the earth in the US is literally electrified with power travelling back to the substation so youâ€™re essentially introducing extra electrical pollution and extra electro-magnetic frequencies into your bed or wherever you happen to be sleeping or standing on this grounding mat when you use grounding or earthing mats in the US.**

Wow! I didn't think that it could be that hard to be grounded... is this a US-only thing? Do you have any references / links / sources?

### Dali Dula — 2013-07-09T15:03:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-mats-better-than-nothing.7583/post-89762>

> [marcus said:](/goto/post?id=89744)
>
> Wow! I didn't think that it could be that hard to be grounded... is this a US-only thing? Do you have any references / links / sources?

Grounding ,or as the Brits call it, earthing can be achieved almost anywhere on EARTH. All residential electric service panels are required to be grounded with a copper or galvanized grounding rod which is driven 6 ft into the earth. Where driving a rod is impossible a bare copper wire loop can be buried around the perimeter of the building. If you have access to your breaker panel you should be able to see a thick bare copper wire and see where it goes. Verify that it is in good condition and it's path to ground is complete.
If you verify good ground this does not mean that all or any of the circuits in the home are properly grounded. If all the wiring in the home was done properly all the circuits will be properly grounded. Being properly grounded does not mean that no magnetic fields or emf are being generated. For under $5 you can buy a circuit tester that plugs into a receptacle and will tell you the status of that circuit. So it's simple to tell if an individual circuit is grounded. It's a bit harder to test lighting circuits which are usually separated from receptacle circuits. Any device with a three prong plug plugged into a properly grounded circuit will be grounded. That being said the device itself ,for instance a microwave oven, may still produce mag field or emf while being properly grounded. Given proper ground path I see no reason why a grounding mat or similar device would not work properly.

### Martin — 2013-07-09T15:38:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-mats-better-than-nothing.7583/post-89763>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=89762)
>
> Grounding ,or as the Brits call it, earthing can be achieved almost anywhere on EARTH. All residential electric service panels are required to be grounded with a copper or galvanized grounding rod which is driven 6 ft into the earth. Where driving a rod is impossible a bare copper wire loop can be buried around the perimeter of the building. If you have access to your breaker panel you should be able to see a thick bare copper wire and see where it goes. Verify that it is in good condition and it's path to ground is complete.
> If you verify good ground this does not mean that all or any of the circuits in the home are properly grounded. If all the wiring in the home was done properly all the circuits will be properly grounded. Being properly grounded does not mean that no magnetic fields or emf are being generated. For under $5 you can buy a circuit tester that plugs into a receptacle and will tell you the status of that circuit. So it's simple to tell if an individual circuit is grounded. It's a bit harder to test lighting circuits which are usually separated from receptacle circuits. Any device with a three prong plug plugged into a properly grounded circuit will be grounded. That being said the device itself ,for instance a microwave oven, may still produce mag field or emf while being properly grounded. Given proper ground path I see no reason why a grounding mat or similar device would not work properly.

One reason is that when you are on a grounding mat or pad, and you are in the vicinity of an electrical field from an appliance, breaker panel, wiring, etc., your body acts as an "antenna", and the fields from the electrical devices pass thru you on their way to earth. I've done some testing with a body voltage meter and confirmed this for myself and no longer ground with a pad on my bed nor at my computer. Instead, I turn off the breakers at night and ground my computer with the USB device outlined by Neuert.

When I thought about plugging into the house's grounding/earthing system in the wiring, I realized how often stray currents can potentially be picked up by the wiring and passed thru you when you tap into all that wiring. Not only voltage for power, but radio frequencies as well, are being picked up by the wiring. And "dirty electricity" from transformers, light ballasts, dimmers, and other devices are all emitting frequencies that can get picked up by home wiring.

I have wondered if one could sleep on a grounded mat with a wire straight to a rod in the ground that was nowhere near any underground power sources, in a room devoid of wiring, and shielded from radio/microwave frequencies, if wouldn't be a good way to get a good night's sleep.

> [MeghanK said:](/goto/post?id=89701)
>
> **
> The problem with this is that when you plug those mats in the US at least, into the wall, they plug-in to the grounding outlets so theyâ€™re grounded and in the US, about 70% of the power that goes into our homes and our buildings travels back to the substation, not through the wires but through the ground. And the earth in the US is literally electrified with power travelling back to the substation so youâ€™re essentially introducing extra electrical pollution and extra electro-magnetic frequencies into your bed or wherever you happen to be sleeping or standing on this grounding mat when you use grounding or earthing mats in the US.**

I know that in the US the "common wire" (usually the white wire that leaves a light, fixure, or other electrical device), used to be connected to the ground circuit. But that is very old technology, and is not legal today. I am surprised at the 70% figure.

But we are speaking of quantum issues, and even if it were 30% or less, it still is a concerning issue.

And the earth in the US is literally electrified with power travelling back to the substation so youâ€™re essentially introducing extra electrical pollution and extra electro-magnetic frequencies into your bed or wherever you happen to be sleeping or standing on this grounding mat when you use grounding or earthing mats in the US.**

### nonchalant — 2013-07-13T18:59:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-mats-better-than-nothing.7583/post-90058>

Well, there is nothing like a good n=1 experiment. I removed my grounding sheet off my magnetico bed for two nights, but I am back to using my grounding sheet as of this morning. My back is demanding it.

Actually, the experiment has been going on for a bit longer. We have been on a road trip for 2 weeks. During that time, I have been ungrounded for 4 nights, properly grounded with a 6 foot deep grounding rod for 2 nights, and the rest of the time grounded with my sheet attached to an 8 inch long tent stake. When we got back home to the magnetico, I decided to remove the grounding sheet. After all, in the past month or so I haven't felt the tiny tingle I used to feel when laying on the grounding sheet, so who knew if it was still working for me?

This morning, though, I couldn't hardly get out of bed, my back was so stiff. I managed to get up and spread out the sheet, and lay back down for 30 minutes. I could feel the wonderful tingle in the small of my back, just where it was needed. It relaxed my back enough so that I felt like getting up for the day. I have already gone back to lay on it for about 10 minutes, to relieve another spasm.

Based on my experience these past 2 weeks, I'm not sure a short (8 inch) spike in the ground is very useful. However I would use the tent stake again, as a temporary measure while traveling. I also found that in a hot environment, the grounding sheet is uncomfortable. The silver inside the threads of the fabric hold in your body heat. I would find myself laying half on and half off, or just touching my hands or feet to it.

Before these past 2 weeks, my back was in excellent shape. The best it has been in years. I hope my back recovers from all this n=1 before I see the chiropractor next week. I'd rather get improvement from the session, not just pain relief.

### MeghanK — 2013-07-13T20:31:05+0300

### tellmisty — 2013-07-16T05:34:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-mats-better-than-nothing.7583/post-90208>

During that time, I have been ungrounded for 4 nights, properly grounded with a 6 foot deep grounding rod for 2 nights, and the rest of the time grounded with my sheet attached to an 8 inch long tent stake. When we got back home to the magnetico, I decided to remove the grounding sheet. > This morning, though, I couldn't hardly get out of bed, my back was so stiff. I managed to get up and spread out the sheet, and lay back down for 30 minutes. I could feel the wonderful tingle in the small of my back, just where it was needed. It relaxed my back enough so that I felt like getting up for the day. I have already gone back to lay on it for about 10 minutes, to relieve another spasm.
>

I also found that in a hot environment, the grounding sheet is uncomfortable. The silver inside the threads of the fabric hold in your body heat. I would find myself laying half on and half off, or just touching my hands or feet to it.
>
> Before these past 2 weeks, my back was in excellent shape. The best it has been in years. I hope my back recovers from all this n=1 before I see the chiropractor next week. I'd rather get improvement from the session, not just pain relief.

I think you might be oversimplifying the grounding phenomenon and not considering the other potential conditions that are important to consider when evaluating the benefits of grounding.

I got a set of test meters from Michael to measure all the different kinds of EMF so I could better-understand how all of this "works." The first thing Michael had me to was measure outside in the middle of my backyard. These numbers essentially represent the "lowest" numbers you can expect to get by removing EMF from your environment. The wheels can fall of the bus here, because if these numbers are too high, nothing you do to remove EMF from your environment will give you the net benefits you are seeking to significantly improve your health.

Another thing we did was cut the breaker to the house, and he had me go upstairs and measure in the middle of the master bedroom. The radio frequency readings we got were high, and there was not really anything I could do about it short of creating some kind of faraday cage (which, incidentally, would also turn the room into a microwave for internal frequencies). Because I was at a higher elevation, my RF readings were higher upstairs than down -- significantly. Ironically the numbers showed up about a foot above my bed. As long as I'm laying flat, I'm out of the RF range. Thankfully.

So here's the clencher: by having grounding sheets on my bed, even those that might be connected to a ground wire, I ran the risk of pulling that RF energy into the copper wiring I was sleeping on, essentially electrifying my entire body while I slept at night. So while the wires ultimately would "dump" that energy into the earth, I would be sitting on top of a major conductor that was pulling in all sorts of other EMFs from the room.

So here's the topper: In February, I slept on grounding sheets a good part of the month. INEXPLICABLY that month, I skipped a period, I started gaining weight at an alarming pace (I'm usually very steady ... I think I put on more than 20 lbs in about 4 weeks -- Imagine my horror -- and my hormones went bonkers. Doctors thought I had premature ovarian failure when my lab results came back). My weight has been very steady for years, and my diet is always clean and pristine. The ONLY condition that had changed for me was the grounding sheets.

I also noticed after a few weeks on the grounding sheets that I was not sleeping well ... I was waking up every couple of hours with hot flashes.

Ironically, during our Q&A call in March or April (March, I think), we were IMing in the chat area and someone else said they skipped a period completely in February, and she thought it was due to the grounding sheets.

So personally, I don't trust them and would caution anyone against "trying" them. The only exception I would make is, if you've measured the room and EMFs are virtually non-existent, you're grounded outside (and there are no sources of EMFs around where you're staked in the ground), there are no EMFs along the trajectory of the copper wire, and you test rigorously with a body meter to ensure you're not accumulating ANY body voltage through the night, then it might be a good choice. Frankly, I think the Magnetico Sleep Pads are a way more solid option if you're serious about creating some health benefits while you sleep.

I am STILL working off the weight I gained four months ago ... it's coming off WAY slower than it came on. I wouldn't wish this on anyone, especially those in this community like me who are working so hard and are so diligent about their health!

Grounding outside directly to the earth is very different from using the mats and copper wiring ... when we're connected directly to the earth, there is no wiring that could be "pulling" or conducting other EMF sources. Incidentally, Michael reports in his anecdotal experience, he has clients who cannot ground barefoot in the grass in high EMF environments, as their bodies pick up the EMF charges (Michael works primarily with people who have significant EMF sensitivities) and they're very uncomfortable. So in his experience, you can't just ground anywhere ... it needs to be a good area with low EMFs.

We are going to start a replay of the EMF Bootcamp this week. I'll have one of the team members come back and post a link here so you can sign up if you're interested.

Great post Misty! Lot's to chew on. # Thread: Those at the top = less stressed
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/those-at-the-top-less-stressed.7585/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2013-07-09T02:48:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/those-at-the-top-less-stressed.7585/post-89726>

[http://www.drlam.com/blog/latest-st...t-the-top-are-not-as-stressed-as-others/3371/](http://www.drlam.com/blog/latest-study-finds-that-those-at-the-top-are-not-as-stressed-as-others/3371/)

Cool quick article about stress cortisol levels and people at the top...those who get to set their own schedules, have financial security etc.

I wonder how farmers would fare in this article, or the mountain men of that history channel show.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-09T03:56:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/those-at-the-top-less-stressed.7585/post-89731>

Those who embrace discomfort and curiosity are the most happy and have the best ASI: They shun short term gains for long term rewards and they do not compare themselves of their results to others.........This is why I say all the time embrace your discomfort and enhance your curiosity and ask questions and dont settle.

### JanSz — 2013-07-09T04:49:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/those-at-the-top-less-stressed.7585/post-89733>

> [Hope said:](/goto/post?id=89726)
>
> [http://www.drlam.com/blog/latest-st...t-the-top-are-not-as-stressed-as-others/3371/](http://www.drlam.com/blog/latest-study-finds-that-those-at-the-top-are-not-as-stressed-as-others/3371/)
>
> Cool quick article about stress cortisol levels and people at the top...those who get to set their own schedules, have financial security etc.
>
> I wonder how farmers would fare in this article, or the mountain men of that history channel show.

> The 148 participants had to offer a description of their jobs, mention the number of workers that reported to them directly or indirectly and also provided a sample of their saliva for a test to measure the cortisol levels (cortisol is the hormone that is indicative of higher or lower levels of stress). To establish a parallel study, researchers brought in 65 people of same age, ethnicity, and gender from non-managerial and non-leadership posts who were subjected to the same tests.
>
> The analysis of the results showed that leaders who have done well in their lives and held commanding positions in their line of work had an increased sense of control and due to the prosperity associated with other benefits, had lower levels of cortisol which confirmed the assessment of their psychological traits of being less stressed.

Usually when people have low saliva cortisol, I would say that they have a problem.
Some call it Adrenal Fatigue, others deficient circadian rhythm.
Add to that low DHEA,saliva and we have a classic problem seen daily.

Yet, when leaders in that study had low saliva cortisol that is taken as positive.

I could use some explanation.

When I had low cortisol I was tired, low energy and sleepy.
Now my cortisol is near top range and I feel good.

When I try to help someone, I often want them to raise their (low) cortisol.

...

### Inger — 2013-07-09T09:09:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/those-at-the-top-less-stressed.7585/post-89745>

Great reminder Hope. I have slipped into one cup/day last days due to stress (so many guests here, not mine, my parents, and I cannot wait until friday when finally peace returns and I can concentrate on my own stuff.. ) and I know it is the wrong decision, I can totally feel it raises my cortisol level. I found this article when I googled coffee and cortisol;

[http://http://www.naturalnews.com/034674\_coffee\_cortisol\_weight\_gain.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/034674_coffee_cortisol_weight_gain.html)

> (NaturalNews) Conventional wisdom about weight loss suggests coffee may be a good addition to a diet plan since it has minimal calories and no fat. While this seems logical, a more holistic view evaluates coffee from the perspective of its influence on the body's ability to metabolize the other food we eat. Recent studies suggest that despite its low calorie count, coffee may actually promote weight gain as well as type 2 diabetes by stimulating cortisol production and insulin resistance.
>
> Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/034674\_coffee\_cortisol\_weight\_gain.html#ixzz2YWcR0T3z](http://www.naturalnews.com/034674_coffee_cortisol_weight_gain.html#ixzz2YWcR0T3z)

> Caffeine stimulates cortisol production

> Regular coffee drinking may increase tolerance to cortisol response
>
> Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/034674\_coffee\_cortisol\_weight\_gain.html#ixzz2YWcfymGC](http://www.naturalnews.com/034674_coffee_cortisol_weight_gain.html#ixzz2YWcfymGC)

> Cortisol promotes fat storage

Now.. regular coffee use cannot be good for anyone with not optimal cortisol levels yet. I have realized especially if I drink one cup in the morning with breakfast it feels not good at all. I need to drik it later, at lunch or so, and very slow. Not all in once. Then I feel not so strong reaction. I still am 100% sure I do me a favor when I do not drink it at all. My well-being increases.

>
> **Those who embrace discomfort and curiosity are the most happy and have the best ASI: They shun short term gains for long term rewards and they do not compare themselves of their results to others.........This is why I say all the time embrace your discomfort and enhance your curiosity and ask questions and dont settle**

Wow. I totally needed this today. Embrace discomfort. Never compare yourself to others. Think, long term rewards. Be curious!
Feels amazing to be reminded of the beautiful truth.  All of a sudden I got my power back.. yay!!!!

### princess_serenity — 2013-07-09T05:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-epi-paleo-if-allergic-to-seafood.7588/post-89739>

Hi!

New to all of this and the first thing i noticed was had to eat ep-paleo. But how do i do the epi paleo diet if im allergic to Oyster and Crustaceans and even some fish?

i react extremely to crabs, lobster and shrimp that i can't stand even 2 pcs before i start to itch and swell in the face so i really dont know how to start with eating epi paleo.

I have:
1\. lots of different allergies - not worth mentioning because they are so many
2\. high cortisol
3\. insomnia
4\. large waistline- (becoming larger)
5\. inability to lose weight - for the past 6 mos i eat mostly meat (skeletal) and veggies (lots of) and have cut off my carbs for meals (except the occassional craving and cookie). this has no effect on my waistline and weight. i am still the same as my former carb eating self.

6\. lifelong constipation - been constipated since i was 2 years old (35 now) but bowel movement has improved for the past 6 mos due to the more veggies diet.
7\. irregular periods + pms
8\. am regularly on benadryl (for the allergies) 25 mg

what i have been taking:
1\. DIM capsules
2\. Alpha lipoic acid
3\. garlic oil
4\. chromium
5\. multivitamin
6\. probiotics (assorted probiotics for a year now)

my blood tests for LDL, cholesterol, trigiycerides, glucose (fasting and random), are perfect. done every 6 months.

Is there any way i can go epi-paleo?

Thanks.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-09T05:58:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-epi-paleo-if-allergic-to-seafood.7588/post-89741>

Thanks.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-09T14:37:29+0300

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-16T23:36:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-eat-epi-paleo-if-allergic-to-seafood.7588/post-153066>

But.. what if you DO have an IgE-Mediated seafood allergy?

It's my understanding that is what constitutes being "truly allergic" based on what I read here -

[http://food.unl.edu/allergy/ige-mediated](http://food.unl.edu/allergy/ige-mediated)

"IgE-mediated food allergies are true food allergies involving an abnormal response of the immune system to one or more specific foods. These reactions are associated with the rapid onset of symptoms – usually within a few minutes to a few hours after the ingestion of the offending food."

.. but please correct me if I'm wrong, because I admittedly am just now learning about all of this as my test results are coming in. Thanks!

Yes, but what about the above, please?

I have an IgE-Mediated seafood allergy which causes anaphylaxis to the extent that even just the air around cooking scallops causes wheezing. The anaphlactic episode that netted me the allergy diagnosiswas set off by clams, so I suspect it is mollusks I am allergic to but I don't want to chance eating anything else until my RAST results are in. At least back then I lived five minutes from a hospital - now it's a full thirty minutes by highway. I can't take the risk.

Right now all I know is the IgE was flagged as high at 143, but the initial items tested were not ones that I reacted to. They initially didn't order the food RAST, which frustrates me still. Hopefully soon here we'll know exactly what all caused this spike.

Back in 2007 my IgE was only 32, so I know that I am not just a person with high IgE as a norm.

Either way, I will not be eating oysters and many other things so I was hoping for some more guidance as how to modify the menu accordingly.
