# Thread: Getting your "Cold" water out of shower, COLDER....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-your-cold-water-out-of-shower-colder.7620/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MeghanK — 2013-07-12T17:48:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-your-cold-water-out-of-shower-colder.7620/post-89962>

Is there anyway to get your cold water colder in your shower? I live in an apartment complex so I don't think they can adjust the water for the entire building but I was wondering if there was anything I could do myself... it only gets to 68ish degrees when it's just on cold ;(

### Lahelada — 2013-07-12T17:58:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-your-cold-water-out-of-shower-colder.7620/post-89966>

I only have a shower as well that is not cold enough. I use ice to run over my body before and after the shower to enhance the effect of the cold water.
I just had a strange idea too just now. If you could fix a colander /strainer in an overhead position and fill it with ice you might get colder water, but it would reduce the flow rate. and only work for a period of time.

### fitness@home — 2013-07-12T21:26:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-your-cold-water-out-of-shower-colder.7620/post-89983>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=89966)

> I just had a strange idea too just now. If you could fix a colander /strainer in an overhead position and fill it with ice you might get colder water, but it would reduce the flow rate. and only work for a period of time.

What a novel idea! If someone rigs this up, please post a picture.

### nicld — 2013-07-13T18:40:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-your-cold-water-out-of-shower-colder.7620/post-90054>

I have that same issue right now but mine comes from my well and can only get it down to 65. I am hoping that is cold enough to make a difference. After vacation I might start adding bags of ice.

Nicole

### GlenPDQ — 2013-07-13T18:58:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-your-cold-water-out-of-shower-colder.7620/post-90057>

How about rigging a highpowered fan to blow down into the shower? The air, as well as the water, would be pulling heat away from your skin. Not sure how well it would work.
Another way might be putting one of those portable AC units (the kind on wheels) in the bathroom and cranking it up. That might work better in smaller bathrooms with less square feet. Can those ice vests be worn in the shower?

### nicld — 2013-07-14T18:06:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-your-cold-water-out-of-shower-colder.7620/post-90103>

Last night it come out at 55 degrees last night and man I felt it.

# Thread: Need Lab Help - Stumped
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-lab-help-stumped.7621/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2013-07-12T17:59:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-lab-help-stumped.7621/post-89967>

I have been having prostate problems for several months and have been tracking some of my sex hormones to see if I can find any correlations.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

A few related labs from Winter, 2012:

Progesterone .2(.2-1.4)
Pregnenalone 47(<151)
SHBG 46.3(19.3-76.4)
DHT 32(30-85)
IGF1 195(75-212)​

I was on arimidex for 1 month in January to lower estradiol. It worked too well as estradiol went from high to a little lower than recommended. In 5 months, estradiol went back up and is now higher than it was originally. I'm finding conflicting info on estradiol and prostate cancer, so I'm not sure whether to try the arimidex again. It really messed with my head and sleep, so I would prefer not taking it again. It is a drug used to treat breast cancer.

Testosterone jumped way up in July (1 month). I think this was from spending a lot of time in sun. Not taking dhea, 7-keto, pregnenalone, testosterone. An interesting side note is my blood pressure dropped significantly during the same time period and I rarely need any medication to keep it in normal range.

When I took the arimidex, DHEA-S went to top of range, and when I stopped, DHEA-s dropped significantly.

I had thought there might be a relationship between dhea-s and PSA level, but my 7/12 lab is just the opposite. I often find that when I am having urinary problems, psa is higher, and vice versa.

The only supplement I am taking that might have a direct effect is progesterone cream. I have been using about 5 g every other day since January. I decided to stop it when I saw my most recent lab results.

So what other labs do I need to figure this out? I need to figure out why estradiol is high and dhea-s is low and what I can do to correct them. I have to make sure I don't take anything that pushes estradiol higher.

Update 1: Additional lab info

Lab on 2/25/13

Tot Chol 262(<200)
Triglicerides 74(<150)
HDL 71(>=40)
LDL 176(0-99) (Doc had me increase ALA in June)

Cortisol is low. RT3 is high. Tried taking cortisol and T3 without success. Decided that good eating and lots of sun would fix me over time.

Update 2: Supplements

Specific supplements I am currently taking to try to address problems.

-   Nettle Root - inhibits binding of testosterone to shbg
-   Saw Palmetto and Zinc - Prevent conversion of testosterone to DHT. Also blocks 5-alpha reductase, aromatase, and stimulates ... nitrous oxide.
-   L-arginine - ED
-   Beta Sitosterol - reduce prostate size

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-12T18:24:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-lab-help-stumped.7621/post-89970>

Huck

what does your Lipid panel look like? also have you had TSH, FREE T4, Free T3 and Reverse T3 run recently? You also might want to consider getting a 23& Me run - then using genetic genie see if you have a MTHFR or COMT Defect which could indicate a methylation and estrogen clearance issue.

Looking at the hormone cascade - it looks like the DHEA your making is getting turned into both Testosterone and Estrogen. You may just have a problem with estrogen clearance .

The following might be a good ask Jack: What does it mean when your DHEA-S is low and all your other steroid hormones are high?

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-07-12T19:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-lab-help-stumped.7621/post-89972>

As JanSz said in this post [http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthre...-TO-ESTROGEN-RATION-22-to-1&p=89950#post89950](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?7609-PROGESTERONE-TO-ESTROGEN-RATION-22-to-1&p=89950#post89950) ....

the results of hormone creams do not register in serum tests.

Saliva or urine tests are more accurate to measure hormones when using creams and/or gels.

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-12T19:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-lab-help-stumped.7621/post-89974>

>
> the results of hormone creams do not register in serum tests.
>

great catch! We've got to remember that each one of is has our own response to things... we all respond differently to different treatments...

### Huck — 2013-07-13T02:57:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-lab-help-stumped.7621/post-90007>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=89984)
>
> Lipid panel

>
> I'm still learning how to interpret labs for men but I think with the full picture - we can see the whole story....

I have most of those, some current and some a couple years old. What I am focused on is anything prostate related. I have symptoms of prostate cancer and doctor recommended a biopsy. I think addressing the dhea/estradiol problem might be key to reducing inflammation and buying some time.

I can enroll in medicare Jan, 2014 and pay nothing for biopsy and any required treatment. Anything I do now will come mostly out-of-pocket as I have a high deductible and co-pay. Doc has me on a sulfur antibiotic that is tearing my stomach up. In the past, antibiotics would reduce symptoms, but not so much this time. She also had me on a colloidal silver tablet that worsened my symptoms, so I stopped that 2 weeks ago. In the past, whenever I have had severe symptoms, one of my steroid hormones has been high (testosterone, dhea-s. estradiol). It happened first when I was using a testosterone cream. It happened again later when I was taking pregnenalone and DHEA. And it happened again when I was taking medication to lower estradiol and my dhea-s went high.

I'm not making a decision based on money alone, but it is a factor. A biopsy will be about $4000 unless I can negotiate a lower price, and that is all out-of-pocket. Then, if I require treatment this year, I could easily be looking at another $5k-$10k. That's on top of insurance cost of over $8k/yr. When I retired, I never planned on such high health insurance costs. I've spent over $100k on insurance and have not been reimbursed for a single expense in 12 years. $100k down the drain.

Pregnenalone 47(<151)
is low
it suggest that you also have low progesterone.
Do not supplement directly with progesterone, specially when it is transdermal type.
Watch also
Cortisol,serum(7:30AM) aim it to be =20

Consider using
pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix 150mg tabs from Nutricology
and
DHEA Micronized Lipid Matrix 50mg tabs from Allergy Research

Use them at wakeup and about 11AM second dose.
Use enough to get:
pregnenolone,serum (do not worry about but monitor)
progesterone (1.4ng/mL up to 5ng/mL but may be higher)
DHEAs(350-490)
Cortisol,serum(7:30AM) aim it to be =20-21
\--------------

You want
Estradiol,sensitive=~20pg/ml
further optimized by good nightly involuntary erections
that level of E2 and progesterone at 5ng/ml would put you at P/E2=~200
you may want to go higher if you are still missing on cortisol.

progesterone should control your E2 naturally and you may not need much of Arimidex.

\-----------------
SHBG= 46.3
that is high, binds most of testosterone, you will need to raise TotalTestosterone and control estrogens (all of them)

Consider doing
Spectracel micronutrients analysis
and
Fatty acids Analysis
follow ed up with optimization of defficiencies.

Save $$ on most supplementah herbs that you are taking.
They are also inhibiting 5aR
that is very hurfull.
Your DHT is already low.
DHT should be converting to 3b-Adiol
3b-Adiol when low may be the reason for your prostate problesm
(supplementation with 3b-Adiol reduced Prostate Cancer in experimental mice)
(This may be new way to treat PC)

/////

How old are you
height
weight
body fat

......

> LDL 176(0-99) (Doc had me increase ALA in June)

Stop ALA, eat oysters, oysters, clams, shrimps and other shellfish
do

> [http://www.mayomedicallaboratories.com/test-catalog/Clinical+and+Interpretive/82042](http://www.mayomedicallaboratories.com/test-catalog/Clinical+and+Interpretive/82042)
> **Test ID: FAPCP Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum**
> [http://www.mayomedicallaboratories.com/test-updates/attachment.php?id=13207](http://www.mayomedicallaboratories.com/test-updates/attachment.php?id=13207)
> mayo medical laboratories
> [http://www.mayomedicallaboratories.com/test-catalog/appendix/performing-locations.html](http://www.mayomedicallaboratories.com/test-catalog/appendix/performing-locations.html)
> Select Your Performing Location
> If you are a new client, an international client, or unsure which performing location to choose, select Rochester.
>
> Rochester
> New England
> Florida (Jacksonville)

/////

Look at this post and couple below:

[http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508)

/

### Huck — 2013-07-21T16:24:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-lab-help-stumped.7621/post-90506>

Thanks JanSz,

I stopped the beta-sitosterol and saw palmetto about a week ago So far so good - no urinary problems.

# Thread: Why does my sleep suck several hours after exercise
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-my-sleep-suck-several-hours-after-exercise.7622/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-07-12T20:03:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-my-sleep-suck-several-hours-after-exercise.7622/post-89975>

I've posted before about how my sleep hasn't been great over the last 10 months but it has improved somewhat over the last couple months.

I now think it's because while on the Reset i have not been working out.

Yesterday at work we played football (mainly lots of short sprints) from 4 to 6pm.

I pushed it to hard, not because I wanted the exercise but for the love of making a play.

It's hard not to, it's kind of addictive

Afterwards into the evening the body was very tired but my sleep was terrible.

I couldn't fall asleep until after 2am.

I originally thought that this had something to do with the high fat but my sleep is improved (not awesome but improved) on high fat with no exercise.

**Could this have something to do with the hypothyroidism?**

Thanks for your input.

BTW it has nothing to do with caffeine as I don't consume any in any form

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-12T21:53:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-my-sleep-suck-several-hours-after-exercise.7622/post-89987>

Increase of cortisol when it should be decreasing as part of the natural circadian cycle. However in your case as a shift worker - you don't have a "natural circadian cycle" yours is discombobulated. So any time you stress (exercise included) your body - it responds with a cortisol response and adrenalin - thus how you can perform at fires at 2am and why your sleep isn't great.

### fireforlunch — 2013-07-12T23:24:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-my-sleep-suck-several-hours-after-exercise.7622/post-89997>

I always appreciate your input.

That makes sense, nevertheless that brings up two questions:

1) why did it only really start when I started eating low carb/high fat?

2) why do the other guys at my work not really have this problem?

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=89987)
>
> Increase of cortisol when it should be decreasing as part of the natural circadian cycle. However in your case as a shift worker - you don't have a "natural circadian cycle" yours is discombobulated. So any time you stress (exercise included) your body - it responds with a cortisol response and adrenalin - thus how you can perform at fires at 2am and why your sleep isn't great.

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-13T03:05:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-my-sleep-suck-several-hours-after-exercise.7622/post-90008>

> [fireforlunch said:](/goto/post?id=89997)
>
> I always appreciate your input.
>
> That makes sense, nevertheless that brings up two questions:
>
> 1) why did it only really start when I started eating low carb/high fat?
>
> 2) why do the other guys at my work not really have this problem?

They Do, they just don't know it...

### Grizz — 2013-08-02T13:03:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-my-sleep-suck-several-hours-after-exercise.7622/post-91366>

My experience confirms this link:
Boron Cures Insomnia:
01/16/2011: Sheryl from Lawrenceville, Ga. Usa replies: "I have had insomnia for years. I have tried everything... Calcium supplements, melatonin, ambien, benedryl and much more. The ONLY thing that has helped has been Borax, taken as suggested by Ted. I started it for other reasons, but the most amazing thing happened. I slept well for the first time in many years. # Thread: IgG vs IgA food testing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/igg-vs-iga-food-testing.7623/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vkiernan — 2013-07-12T20:58:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/igg-vs-iga-food-testing.7623/post-89978>

I have now had the Enterolab (IgA) and the ELISA (IgG) food testing. Eggs and dairy showed very strong reactions to on both so they are a no go. But, on the Enterolabs test, it showed a reaction to pork, chicken and tuna but the ELISA showed no reaction. I realize that these tests are from different perspectives within our bodies but now I'm not sure if I can eat those meats or not. My question to anyone out there is what is the real difference and how do I interpret?

Thanks for any input!

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-12T21:39:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/igg-vs-iga-food-testing.7623/post-89985>

Here's what I found:
"IgE, IgG, and IgA are acronyms for the three different kinds of antibodies produced by the immune system in allergic reactions to food Conventional allergy testing looks for IgE reactions only. These types of reactions typically occur immediately after contact with or ingestion of the allergen, and in some cases can cause serious, even fatal, health problems. Potential IgE reactions include swelling of the lips and tongue, hives, bloating, abdominal pain, or sudden diarrhea. These are the reactions that people usually think of when they hear the word allergy. However, IgE reactions can also be delayed, and can lead to many other symptoms not traditionally recognized as being caused by food allergies.

The problem with the standard food allergy tests, run by most allergists, is that **most food allergies are not IgE reactions, but are rather IgG reactions or sometimes IgA reactions**, such as in celiac disease. T**he IgG and IgA reactions usually show up hours or even days after ingestion of the allergen**. They are generally not nearly as dramatic as the more severe IgE reactions, and usually result in “mere” constipation, diarrhea, bloating, gas and abdominal pain. All three antibodies are important, and food allergy testing should include them or the cause of IBS may be missed."

I would say that for the foods you have an IgG response to, you should probably eliminate them. Same for the IgA responses. You're having a different response at a different IgG/IgA response depending on the food. some of which are causing a response to both IgG/IgA.

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-12T21:41:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/igg-vs-iga-food-testing.7623/post-89986>

here's a good article about food allergies, and sensitivities... [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685801/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2685801/)

### vkiernan — 2013-07-12T22:14:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/igg-vs-iga-food-testing.7623/post-89990>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=89985)
>
> Here's what I found:
> "IgE, IgG, and IgA are acronyms for the three different kinds of antibodies produced by the immune system in allergic reactions to food Conventional allergy testing looks for IgE reactions only. These types of reactions typically occur immediately after contact with or ingestion of the allergen, and in some cases can cause serious, even fatal, health problems. Potential IgE reactions include swelling of the lips and tongue, hives, bloating, abdominal pain, or sudden diarrhea. These are the reactions that people usually think of when they hear the word allergy. However, IgE reactions can also be delayed, and can lead to many other symptoms not traditionally recognized as being caused by food allergies.
>
> The problem with the standard food allergy tests, run by most allergists, is that **most food allergies are not IgE reactions, but are rather IgG reactions or sometimes IgA reactions**, such as in celiac disease. T**he IgG and IgA reactions usually show up hours or even days after ingestion of the allergen**. They are generally not nearly as dramatic as the more severe IgE reactions, and usually result in “mere” constipation, diarrhea, bloating, gas and abdominal pain. All three antibodies are important, and food allergy testing should include them or the cause of IBS may be missed."
>
> I would say that for the foods you have an IgG response to, you should probably eliminate them. Same for the IgA responses. You're having a different response at a different IgG/IgA response depending on the food. some of which are causing a response to both IgG/IgA.

Thanks much! I had read that if you have an IgA response that you should "never" eat that food again and with the IgG, you might be able to later. Hoping that's not entirely true.

# Thread: Pondering upon "People who live in this pathway can run a marathon with no training."

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pondering-upon-people-who-live-in-this-pathway-can-run-a-marathon-with-no-training.7627/post-90018>

Having read this blog post about a year ago... I am pondering...

"People who live in this pathway can run a marathon with no training." - cite from Cold Thermogenesis 6 - the ancient pathway

This is true from a "energy supply perspective". But what about your bones, tendons and ligaments? If you aren't used to such a load (m-a-r-a-t-h-o-n! - 26.2 miles! - 42.2 km!), you will end up in pain and inflammation. You will ruin your body's passive structures.

Thus, "running a marathon" is just a metaphor for outstanding endurance performance, right?

I haven't run a marathon (yet?), but several (fast) 10km races and half marathons. I think I know what I am talking about... nonetheless I would appreciate the idea of adapting my body's passive structures by cold adaptation.

# Thread: Handling of muscle trigger points and / or tighten up muscles - maybe a energy metabo
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/handling-of-muscle-trigger-points-and-or-tighten-up-muscles-maybe-a-energy-metabo.7628/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### marcus — 2013-07-13T07:44:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/handling-of-muscle-trigger-points-and-or-tighten-up-muscles-maybe-a-energy-metabo.7628/post-90019>

I love endurance sports. I know this kind of sports isn't benefical for health and longevity... I am in a transition period finding **my** way of being healthy and happy. Anyway... after my training session I sometimes end up with (the day after) tight muscles. It's no muscle soreness but trigger points ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trigger\_point](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trigger_point)).

**What is the cause?** Could it be just muscle overload? Maybe, but not in my case. It's independent of effort - aerobic or anaerobic. There must be something different. Is it a metabolism issue? **Maybe production of too much ROS in cells? Maybe lack of micronutrients?** I suppose it is in this area. Starting about four weeks ago I am on a (zero) carb diet now. Plus avoiding EMF as much as possible, living circadian cycles, reducing endurance training (shifiting towards heavy lifting exercises), prefer seafoods (oysters, salmon, sea veggies), grounding, and don't be to serious at all. This might be a solution but I expect adaptation needs muuuuuuuuuuuuuch time.

Anyone gained experience in this field and shares it with me?

(I hope my english doesn't suck at all... sorry guys - don't hesitate to correct me)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-13T14:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/handling-of-muscle-trigger-points-and-or-tighten-up-muscles-maybe-a-energy-metabo.7628/post-90030>

many causes but I would say it is intracellular water loss in the cells cyto architecture that is the real cause.

What does cause an intracellular water loss? Any ideas how to fix this issue?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/handling-of-muscle-trigger-points-and-or-tighten-up-muscles-maybe-a-energy-metabo.7628/post-90078>

EMF, fluoride, lack of sodium, not drinking enough good water.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/handling-of-muscle-trigger-points-and-or-tighten-up-muscles-maybe-a-energy-metabo.7628/post-90441>

fascia is composed of one of our main semiconductors.......collagen. This is where many nanotubes are found. It is also where the meridians are found. With out water fascia tightens. Muscles can not work.........without water we lose current and energy.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-20T18:31:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/handling-of-muscle-trigger-points-and-or-tighten-up-muscles-maybe-a-energy-metabo.7628/post-90442>

fascia is part of the tensgrity system in the zero entropy machine called life.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/handling-of-muscle-trigger-points-and-or-tighten-up-muscles-maybe-a-energy-metabo.7628/post-90818>

Another thing is the fascia's over compensating. Causes for that is a lack of endurance or strength, posture imbalances or incorrect breath and movement engagement.

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bNPgqS3EfRw](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bNPgqS3EfRw)

[http://i42.tinypic.com/301fqd3.jpg](http://i42.tinypic.com/301fqd3.jpg) There's a better image about. This is just something I quickly done up in MS paint. Black and grey arrows are output, pink is direct support, clear is other. red is muscle green is fascia

Nothing wrong with it because it's one way the body adapts. But if it's overworked the fascia becomes an issue (saw/painful) locally or elsewhere depending where is weakest.

I am in the process of preparing my home in Alexandria, VA for sale and moving away from what I assume to be an EMF-saturated area.

We will be looking for a place in the vicinity of Pensacola.

We could plan to do our initial house-hunting excursion next week, something we have had to delay due to home-upgrade contractor problems (of course, all depends on airline arrangements). It would be a great pleasure to attend a meet and greet.

# Thread: Waking up stressed angry or panicked between 5 or 6 am
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2013-07-13T15:50:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90038>

Good morning ,

I have been taking Melatonin this week to break a sleep cycle of waking up between 5 and 6 . Apart from the first day the pattern continues . I am not planning to take Melatonin beyond a week but would have hoped to get a minivacation.
My routine currently is dinner at 8 and bedtime at 11.59 . For the moment I cannot change it to earlier. Sunrise is currently around 8.
My question is what could be happening at this time around 5 or 6 ? Or what could be causing this "fight or flight syndrome" that comes on immediately on waking ? Has Cortisol production already kicked in again at that time?
Many thanks for any answers/speculations.

### Lahelada — 2013-07-13T15:55:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90039>

Thinking about it it could be as early as 4 am if it makes a difference.

### fitness@home — 2013-07-13T17:04:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90043>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=90039)
>
> Thinking about it it could be as early as 4 am if it makes a difference.

Not sure of what the mechanism at work is, but there are times that I come awake with a roar in my ears (for want of a better description) between 2:30- 4 am. I am on different sleep schedule than you though. Hit the sack at 8:30-ish and up at 4 am.

### nonchalant — 2013-07-13T18:18:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90050>

I wonder about pulsed EMF.

### Lahelada — 2013-07-14T17:10:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90099>

Thank you for the pointers everyone. Nonchalant, I had never heard of pulsed EMf so that was an interesting read.
I am afraid that going to bed beforehand is really not on my radar unless I absolutely have to. Last night I went to bed at 11.30 and for now that is my limit.
However a surprising result. We live in a quiet area but that might just be augmenting any extraneous noises at night, like the cats that congregate at the corner at the other side of the road the rubbish collection at night or the cacophony of wild parrots first thing in the morning, very early in the morning. Anyway it could be anything that is not rare enough to wake me up yet subconsciously register. ThatÂ´s the theory. So I went out to buy ear plugs yesterday, went to bed at 11.30 pm and slept for 9 hours without stress. I woke up once and decided it was not time and was asleepagain in the space of 5 minutes. LetÂ´s see if it perpetuates itself.

### PrimalPam — 2013-07-14T18:02:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90102>

\# 1: Dr. K. does not advocate exogenous melatonin. If you search the blogs, I'm sure you can find it, but I threw out my melatonin after I read his stance on it.
\# 2: You need to figure out a way to have your last meal earlier. By 6 or 7 pm, at the latest. These days, I eat "dinner" (extremely light) around 4-5 pm, and then no food afterwards.
\# 3: Bedtime should be closer to 10 pm. Someway, somehow.
\# 4: Turn off all artificial lights at dusk. No t.v., no computer, no glancing at the cellphone.

Very best; good sleep is crucial.

### motivatedhealthseeker — 2013-07-14T18:46:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90109>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=90038)

> Good morning ,
>
> I have been taking Melatonin this week to break a sleep cycle of waking up between 5 and 6 . Apart from the first day the pattern continues . I am not planning to take Melatonin beyond a week but would have hoped to get a minivacation.
> My routine currently is dinner at 8 and bedtime at 11.59 . For the moment I cannot change it to earlier. Sunrise is currently around 8.
> My question is what could be happening at this time around 5 or 6 ? Or what could be causing this "fight or flight syndrome" that comes on immediately on waking ? Has Cortisol production already kicked in again at that time?
> Many thanks for any answers/speculations.

Possible low cortisol resulting in adrenaline rush? Do you have adrenal fatigue?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-15T01:29:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90122>

> \# 2: You need to figure out a way to have your last meal earlier. By 6 or 7 pm, at the latest. These days, I eat "dinner" (extremely light) around 4-5 pm, and then no food afterwards.
> \# 3: Bedtime should be closer to 10 pm. Someway, somehow.
> \# 4: Turn off all artificial lights at dusk. No t.v., no computer, no glancing at the cellphone.

Bingo!!!!!

### Lahelada — 2013-07-15T16:49:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90147>

Thank you everyone for your responses. Thank you Dr Kruse for confirming that I have no choice but to.....if I want...
Motivated healthseeker,yes to adrenal fatigue but mending ever so slowly.

### motivatedhealthseeker — 2013-07-15T18:40:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90161>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=90038)
>
> Good morning ,
>
> I have been taking Melatonin this week to break a sleep cycle of waking up between 5 and 6 . Apart from the first day the pattern continues . I am not planning to take Melatonin beyond a week but would have hoped to get a minivacation.
> My routine currently is dinner at 8 and bedtime at 11.59 . For the moment I cannot change it to earlier. Sunrise is currently around 8.
> My question is what could be happening at this time around 5 or 6 ? Or what could be causing this "fight or flight syndrome" that comes on immediately on waking ? Has Cortisol production already kicked in again at that time?
> Many thanks for any answers/speculations.

Hello Lahelada
Correct me if I am wrong anyone but I think taking melatonin at night when you are already low in cortisol might not a good thing. A saliva test allows you to see a map of the cotrisol rhythm to see when you are low and high etc. Some people have high cortisol at night that doesnt allow them to fall asleep and some are too low in cortisol at night which also hinders sound sleep. Cortisol aids with blood sugar balance so when you dont have enough cortisol available at night to signal glycogen breakdown, body produces adrenaline instead which wakes you up. High protein and fat snack before bedtime is recommended to decease chances of low blood sugar at night but if you are doing the leptin reset then I am not sure what Dr Kruse would recommend.

### Lahelada — 2013-07-16T19:17:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-stressed-angry-or-panicked-between-5-or-6-am.7630/post-90238>

> [motivatedhealthseeker said:](/goto/post?id=90161)
>
> Hello Lahelada
> Some people have high cortisol at night that doesnt allow them to fall asleep and some are too low in cortisol at night which also hinders sound sleep. Cortisol aids with blood sugar balance so when you dont have enough cortisol available at night to signal glycogen breakdown, body produces adrenaline instead which wakes you up. High protein and fat snack before bedtime is recommended to decease chances of low blood sugar at night but if you are doing the leptin reset then I am not sure what Dr Kruse would recommend.

That was highly useful and clear. Thank you ! I will follow up on that and experiment .

Last nightÂ´s sleep was interesting. I ditched the melatonin, it was only meant as a very short term crutch anyway and woke up again at 5, stressed and with a feeling of having eaten 3 plates of soup all at once and freezing at the same time in other parts. I stayed up for an hour without strong light and went back to bed and slept another 5 hours. In other words to sleep periods of 5 hours each. I kicked out the coffee and will see if I can turn back theh clock a little with going to bed before 12
Last night it was 11.30.

### MeghanK — 2013-07-13T18:01:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.7632/post-90047>

I had my magnesium tested last summer and it was 2.2 with reference range of 1.7-2.6... How much Magnesium do you recommend? I usually spray Ancient Magnesium oil on me which says that 8 sprays = 100mg of elemental magnesium.... Ingredients: water, Genuine Zechstein magnesium chloride.... then take 2 tsp. of Natural Calm which is ionic magnesium citrate (created from a highly absorbable proprietary blend of citric acid and magnesium carbonate) which yields 350mg.... so total for me probably like 550mg???? I'm 5'3 1/2" about 95-100lbs....... or should I not even supplement because of my level.....

### HoneyChild — 2013-07-13T18:42:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.7632/post-90055>

I'm guessing that you got a serum test rather than an RBC which is the one you really want.

[http://gotmag.org/how-to-restore-magnesium/](http://gotmag.org/how-to-restore-magnesium/)

It's generally suggested to take you weight in lbs, multiply by 5 and this gives you the rough amount of mag in mg to take. So you'd be wanting to take 500mg however ultimately you want to go based on symptom relief and to have your RBC mag over 6 as per the link above.

### nonchalant — 2013-07-13T19:08:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.7632/post-90061>

I supplemented with 1000 mg of magnesium daily (inside of a chelated magnesium) for about 3 years, and then got a magnesium RBC test. I scored a 5.8, which I thought was great at the time, based on the range. But I will still get a twitchy eyelid if I forget my mag. So I think I am just on the border of being ok.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-06-23T02:35:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.7632/post-244120>

When somebody tells you that taking supplemental magnesium is safe and always makes sense you might want to vet that information carefully. Those people usually have a secondary gain issue in selling you a pill or supplement you may not need. This is superfluous treatment and leads to a profit of the profiteer. Let me give you an example.......Magnesium cannot work without sunlight. Do you know why? Sunlight reverses the photosynthetic process in animals. Photosynthesis, in plants, takes CO2 and H20 and makes sugar. In fact, the entire food web links to photosynthesis from the plant/marine webs linked to sunlight. Let me clear.......THE ENTIRE FOOD WEB links to the sun because of this one reaction. None of your food gurus tell you this when they sell you their books or ideas. Ever wonder why? Because they do not realize it or they don't want you to realize it so they can take advantage of your lack of knowledge or you laziness to educate yourself. You, as an animal with mitochondria, reverse the photosynthetic reaction and turn sugars and fats into CO2 and water. So mitochondria MAKE water from the full oxidation of food via the TCA cycle. Oxidizing glucose alone is not a full combustion process so not enough water is made. Is this why people have a magnesium problem when they eat a high carb low-fat diet in a non-tropical region? YEP. The reason is simple to understand when you look at the process from the perspective of how nature uses photosynthesis........you need more sun to make more water in your matrix to allow magnesium to operate as it should in a sunlit environment. In fact, the amount of sun you live in determines the type of food you SHOULD be eating locally. Today that effect is BROKEN by how humans grow and buy their food. It is no longer controlled by the solar cycles and this is why problems occur. Taking exogenous magnesium DOES nothing to solve this.......it actually makes it WORSE. Did you know that magnesium is hydrophilic in biochemistry? It means it ONLY works when water is present in the aqueous environment. In a person with mitochondrial damage OR A CIRCADIAN DEFECT, not enough water is made in our matrix and taking exogenous Mg in this case can be toxic for a cell. This can stimulate apoptosis and stem cell depots to deplete themselves of your life force more quickly than nature needs it to happen. Magnesium men salespeople never tell you this information......because they act like they do not know it, or they really are this ignorant. Your choice on that viewpoint about them. Most supplement takers and lemmings do not know this either, hence why they are so gullible in becoming a customer of the pill pusher......Today, I care enough to educate your why their ideas are BAD for you because most of you have a circadian arrhythmia from the action of blue light on melanopsin and Vitamin A in your skin. Caveat emptor on who is your expert on magnesium.

99% of the magnesium sold is complete garbage based on actual lab tests. I've only found 1 that is safe and actually works. Taking a boat load of Mag without K+, natural sea salt and Ca- isn't a good idea. You need a minimum of 6 grams of K+ per day. Good luck eating 50 bananas

RBC Mag Testing: The problem with this is that magnesium is not **IN** your blood. In fact, 99% of the magnesium in the body is in the **cells**; while only a mere **1%** of your the body’s **total** magnesium is in the blood, making a blood test for magnesium nearly worthless

better test would be: **Sublingual epithelial cell testing**

.

### John Schumacher — 2020-12-11T23:03:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium.7632/post-293804>

Just a note on magnesium -

-   Jack says:
    1\. Key point before answer it........if you are dehydrated and loaded in an EMF environment Mg DOES NOTHING TO HELP YOU.
    2\. _**Magnesium L-Threonate**_ is best for neural issues........EMF is a neural issue because the brain has more mitochondria than any other organ by a long shot.
    3\. orotate is best for the Heart.
    4\. Malate is best for the muscles of the body
    5\. Mg citrate is best for the gut
    6\. When you take them only matters if you are also taking Vitamin D. Do not take Vitamin D with Mg......if you take D make sure your Mg is notwithin 2 hrs of the dose. Mg cn be used round the clock with little issue.

I wrote:
A hydrated Mg2+ ion has a relatively robust innermost hydration shell with six bound water molecules and a second hydration layer. Fully hydrated Mg2+ ions bind to RNA.

We know the release of phosphate from the nucleotide binding pocket of a myosin motor occurs only after step-wise hydration of Mg2+ by four water molecules. [https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/817254v1.full.pdf](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/817254v1.full.pdf)

With all the talk about DDW, without first thinking about hydrated alkaline metal ions and their behavior in human plasma, it seems silly to me. **_I believe_** we need to first understand cellular hydration both externally to the phospholipid biliary within the phospholipid biliary and within the cytoplasm.

_For example:_

Gas-Phase Reactions of Hydrated Alkaline Earth Metal Ions, M2+ (H2O)_n_ (M = Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba and _n_ = 4–7), With Benzene [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1401488/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1401488/) Ion transport processes across cellular membranes are governed by the role of competitive intramolecular and intermolecular interactions between solvent molecules, proteins or peptides, and the ions of interest. The transfer of an ion through a hydrophobic environment can be effected by two mechanisms. The ion-carrier mechanism involves naturally occurring multidentate ligands known as ionophores. These ligands can arrange to form cavities with higher selectivity toward a specific ion in solution. Ionophores, such as valinomycin and nonactin, bind potassium ions preferentially over the other alkali metals. Once the ion–ionophore complex is formed, the outer hydrophobic surface of the “carrier” facilitates the transport into or across the nonpolar environment. The second ion transport mechanism involves channel-forming ionophores, like the gramicidins, which form hydrophilic pathways extending across a hydrophobic membrane allowing the passage of ions. The selectivity observed in solution for complexes between ionophores and metal ions is a consequence of the small differences between the stability constants of the cations with water and those of the cations with the ionophores.

Hydrated Magnesium Cations Mg+(H2O)n, n ≈ 20–60, Exhibit Chemistry of the Hydrated Electron in Reactions with O2 and CO2 [https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/jp206140k](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/jp206140k) Ion–molecule reactions of Mg+(H2O)n, n ≈ 20–60, with O2 and CO2 are studied by Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry. O2 and CO2 are taken up by the clusters. Both reactions correspond to the chemistry of hydrated electrons (H2O)n–. Density functional theory calculations predicted that the solvation structures of Mg+(H2O)16 contain a hydrated electron that is solvated remotely from a hexa-coordinated Mg2+. Ion–molecule reactions between Mg+(H2O)16 and O2 or CO2 are calculated to be highly exothermic. Initially, a solvent-separated ion pair is formed, with the hexa-coordinated Mg2+ ionic core being well separated from the O2•– or CO2•–. Rearrangements of the solvation structure are possible and produce a contact-ion pair in which one water molecule in the first solvation shell of Mg2+ is replaced by O2•– or CO2•–.

Magnesium is an electrolyte responsible for keeping potassium inside and sodium outside of the cell. Magnesium is found in abundance inside ATP cells (the body's energy cells) and is also involved with the contraction of muscle, nerve conduction and helps with cell division and repair. [http://physicaldimensionsihg.com/the-importance-of-electrolytes-and-hydration](http://physicaldimensionsihg.com/the-importance-of-electrolytes-and-hydration) Potassium is involved with the acid-base regulation keeping potassium levels regulated will help to buffer the lactic acid build up in muscle tissue.

Mg2+ transport proteins to eukaryotes and across the cell membrane. The CorA family of magnesium transporters is the primary Mg2+ uptake system of most prokaryotes [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3836678/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3836678/)

We have two hormones which are very critical to our health

-   "Vitamin" D
    -   [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?posts/29503](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?posts/295030)
    -   [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?posts/293879](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?posts/293879)
    -   [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?posts/293886](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?posts/293886)
-   Melatonin
    -   [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...late-the-pineals-secretion-of-melatonin.25345](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/how-does-darkness-stimulate-the-pineals-secretion-of-melatonin.25345)

    -   [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...prevents-infection-by-novel-coronavirus.25514](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/melatonin-produced-in-the-lungs-prevents-infection-by-novel-coronavirus.25514)
    -   [https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?posts/290958](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?posts/290958)

So we know:​

-   Melatonin is the hormone of darkness [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4334454/ ->](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4334454/) The pineal gland receives input from postganglionic fibers, leading to the release of noradrenaline and increased production of cyclic AMP, thus activating the enzyme AANAT, mentioned above, which is critical to the production of melatonin.

-   Circadian rhythm is tightly coupled to our SCN response to sunlight [https://optimalklubs.com/reality22-sunlight-codes-for-darkness/](https://optimalklubs.com/reality22-sunlight-codes-for-darkness/)

-   Longer exposure to darkness leads to the increase of serum melatonin [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2319417016000238 -](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2319417016000238) _not supplementation._

-   The biomass precursors for building melatonin are
    -   tryptophan,

    -   methionine,

    -   inositol,

    -   magnesium,

    -   vitamins: B-2, B6 & D
-   The inhibitor is any form of Folic Acid

-   Melatonin can be used for the prevention and treatment of all cancers [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5503661/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5503661/)

The question: _Do we need to supplement these two hormones?_

Perhaps if our values are so low we are in danger of disease or cancer.
However, the preferred way to increase these is with the sun & darkness.​

If you don't mind, I will get back to you about krill oil (DHA) and choline. Generally speaking, these two nutrients are very important for your brain and neuro function. It takes around 7 years to replace half of these type of cells -> [neurogenesis](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6747551/).

Testing the levels of your hormones will give you a diagnostic tool.
Clinicians are "taught" differentiation techniques to help them better guess at what might be "really" wrong, but a good diagnosis comes from all the information and lab results are vital.
So for you & me, when we can afford getting lab information, it is a great help in determining our next steps towards "health improvement".

### CTforlife — 2013-07-16T02:33:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-to-rewarm.7635/post-90193>

The recommendation to eat before CT is for beginners. I still eat before CT, but that is how my schedule is set up. I suggest that you eat a high protein/fat meal before you CT in the evenings. When I first started CT it was in the evenings, and I would get sooo "cold" afterwards that I had to take a warm shower in fear of death. Now temperature is a variable to me like everything else in my environment, I adapt to survive.
Yes it is "normal" for a warm adapted mammal to "feel" cold after they CT.
I would consider either changing your schedule and CT in the morning, or become a fat burning furnace. It's really your choice.
It takes time to adapt to the cold, and it definitely takes a lot of time to EMBRACE the cold. The more details you have correct, the faster this "process" happens.
I also would highly suggest you eat a lot of omega 3 rich seafood before you CT. When I "adapted", I ate more salmon than you think was sane. But then again my hs crp and o3 to o6 ratio were worse than you have, and I don't know anything about you.
Improving the engine in all of your "cars" or cells, as smart people call them, is also a wise decision.
The closer your environment is to an actual winter, the faster you will adapt. Following the Leptin Rx and eating Epi-Paleo are some good starting points.

Time is valuable. The more time you "donate" to CT, the more time it "gives" back. After some time, you won't feel cold after arising from 50-55 degrees F water before going to bed. It will calm you down and make you sleepy after a while in the evenings. Change around the duration of your CT sessions until you don't "feel" cold afterwards. Soon enough, and you will EMBRACE the cold.
Sorry if this has spelling/grammatical errors, typed this out really quick.

### nicld — 2013-07-16T03:28:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-to-rewarm.7635/post-90199>

Thanks for all the advice. Maybe getting down to 55Â° and over 30 minutes once the tub is filled in just over a week was a little to fast. I might keep the water a bit warmer tonight and not stay in as long. One good thing is I do not want the ac on tight away at night. It also is only really noticeable once I am in bed but not so much walking around.

It is so weird to me, the freeze baby, that I can sit in a tub of cold cold water and it feels good.

I do eat high protein before creating and a have this blended frozen drink right before too. Blend some stevia, couple of frozen strawberries, coconut oil with some ice and water. It hits the spot right after dinner.

I will also try the colder showers in the morning during the week if I have ate and in the mornings on the weekend.

### CTforlife — 2013-07-16T03:31:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-to-rewarm.7635/post-90202>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=90199)
>
> I do eat high protein before creating and a have this blended frozen drink right before too. Blend some stevia, couple of frozen strawberries, coconut oil with some ice and water. It hits the spot right after dinner.

### Hope — 2013-07-16T03:38:44+0300

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=90105)

>
> I take my cold bath before bed and cannot fall asleep due to trying to warm back up. My core does not feel cold, just the skin.
>
> I am going to try and take it a bit earlier and see if that helps but any suggestions would be great.
>
> Nicole

I have heard Jack say on a QA earlier that the worst time to do CT is before bed...I can't remember why. But it's what he said. I think he said it's better to do it earlier in the evening....hope that helps. Sadly I don't remember which QA it was. I just made a mental note never to do it before bed.

### nicld — 2013-07-16T16:31:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-to-rewarm.7635/post-90233>

I will read more of the CT posts and I think i am following what he said in the first one. Eat a protein and fat before and drink ice cold water.

I was able to take it a bit earlier last night and did not let the water get as cold and bed time was much better. I was hoping to get in a cold shower this morning but just ran out of time. I will try to plan a little better tomorrow and get a longer one in before work since I will be leaving for vacation and not have access to a bath tub but can still take long cold showers (not as good as a bath in my opinion).

This is still so weird to me to sit in a cold bath but still have the feeling of comfort and warmth.

# Thread: Motion sickness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motion-sickness.7636/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2013-07-14T18:21:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motion-sickness.7636/post-90106>

The more I think of motion sickness, the more I think it may not be a malady. It seems to be a reasonable response to a disruption in our sense of direction.

I'm talking about the people who cannot read a book while in the back seat of a car. If they look out the window, however, they are fine. Or if they close their eyes, they are fine. Their brain cannot resolve the incongruity, however, between the stillness that they see (the open book) and the motion of the car (or perhaps the changes in direction). Same for airplane and boat movements. I'm not talking about stormy weather or turbulent movements, just gentle travel that bothers some of us.

Would better mitochondrial support help these people? Or is their response completely normal, and the only resolution is to deaden their sense of direction so that they perceive no incongruity? Are the rest of us, that do not experience nausea, missing a good innate sense of direction?

### fitness@home — 2013-07-14T19:36:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motion-sickness.7636/post-90113>

I'd like to know the answer to this. As a child and teenager, I used to read in the car all the time without nausea. As an adult, it now gives me motion sickness.

### HoneyChild — 2013-07-14T19:39:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motion-sickness.7636/post-90114>

This might give you a clue...
**
Motion Sickness, Stress and the Endocannabinoid System**

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0010752](http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0010752)

### nonchalant — 2013-07-14T19:40:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motion-sickness.7636/post-90115>

Ooh, love those endocannabinoids!
Perhaps when one eats foods with cannaboids (dairy, wheat) they experience problems with motion sickness?

### nonchalant — 2013-07-14T19:45:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/motion-sickness.7636/post-90116>

Excess cannaboids from foods means you aren't good at manufacturing your own?

# Thread: D-Ribose helps with Calorie Restriction
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose-helps-with-calorie-restriction.7638/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2013-07-15T16:27:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose-helps-with-calorie-restriction.7638/post-90145>

Okay I have been reading up on recycling ATP and was wandering, if someone is calorie restricting, would d-ribose mean they would get more energy from the same amount of food thanks to ATP's being recycled? Or would it just make the current energy more available?

Still trying to wrap my head around it,
thanks, Sean.

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-15T17:22:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose-helps-with-calorie-restriction.7638/post-90150>

IMPO - you can't force calorie restricting. it has to happen naturally with CT and as you move into the PPP as a fat burner, and have proper hydration.

I use d-ribose to help support my mitochondria b/c of a history of migraines. as I've entered and stayed in the PPP, leveraged CT and Grounding I've found I have less need for 3 meals/day. It doesn't happen everyday, and its not just related to using d-ribose. its a combination of multiple things.

D-ribose, lets your body convert ADP to ATP at a faster rate b/c it is the base sugar that helps form ATP. so it increases energy availability. However, you're not going to see a decrease in food consumption until you are correctly hydrated, and in the PPP. b/c you need fat, water and d-ribose to enter the PPP to allow your mitochondria to leverage the ATP to unfold proteins correctly to get to the zero state entropy phase. from my understanding you can be in the PPP , and leverage d-ribose to make the ATP that unfold the proteins to allow you to use water as your fuel source. however, you're not going to reach that zero state entropy until you're no longer dehydrated. its when you start to get close to the zero state entropy is when you'll begin to see Calorie restriction as a result.

Jack can correct me if I've missed the mark... but this is how I understand it.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-15T18:42:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose-helps-with-calorie-restriction.7638/post-90162>

Re read CT 7 and CT 8.........they cover this in detail

# Thread: HELP: weight loss and low leptin levels
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-weight-loss-and-low-leptin-levels.7652/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Trinity — 2013-07-17T00:04:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-weight-loss-and-low-leptin-levels.7652/post-90244>

Hi guys,

I am posting this question on behalf of my partner. He is a 41 year old male with a history of Hashimotos thyroid disease diagnosed approximately 3 years ago. Although a healthy weight when younger, he has battled obesity for the past 10 years. At his highest, he weighed around ~247. He managed to loose nearly 40 pounds over the past year after finally getting his thyroid optimized with cytomel. That weight loss put him right around 207 pounds, but a DEXA scan revealed that he was still obese with a body fat of 27%. His weight loss stalled out around 207 pounds and since then he has had increasing difficulty maintaining his weight. Within the past 3 months, he has actually put back on 8-10 pounds. After telling him about Dr. K's website and the role of leptin, he convinced his endocrinologist to run a leptin level. We expected it to be high because he is still obese, but were surprised when it came back extremely low.

The reference range for adult males is given as 1.8 - 19.9 ng / mL. His level was 3.5 ng / mL. His endocrinologist felt that leptin level was way too low given his obese body fat level, but did not have any suggestions about how to improve the situation.

From my research into leptin, I suspect that his low leptin level is a result of his 40 pound weight loss. It is well documented that dieting lowers lepin levels and that this lowering likely places the body into a "starvation mode" that encourages a regain of lost weight. Even though he is still obese after a 40 lb weight loss, it appears that his remaining fat is not producing enough leptin to maintain normal metabolic function. Recently he has also started showing signs of adrenal fatigue and this has required him to lower the optimal cytomel dosage that he has been on without any problems for over a year. He does not have a prior history of adrenal fatigue and I suspect this is related to the leptin issue as well.

The question here is how do we raise his leptin and improve his hormonal function before:
1) he regains the 40 pounds that he lost
2) the low leptin "starvation mode" response leads to full blown adreanal fatigue and a further inability to tolerate his thyroid meds.

As far as I know, leptin injections are not available. Would cycloset be a good option for him? Or is there a better way to tackle this problem?

Thanks,
Trinity

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-17T00:07:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-weight-loss-and-low-leptin-levels.7652/post-90245>

I would say his best bet would be to follow the Leptin reset, focusing on circadian cycles (light/seasonal foods) as well as CT and grounding while eating Epipaleo.

### caroline — 2013-07-17T02:05:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-weight-loss-and-low-leptin-levels.7652/post-90253>

Great advice from Shijin.....has he checked the easy start guides on the home page?

### Anna — 2013-07-17T12:38:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-weight-loss-and-low-leptin-levels.7652/post-90272>

Hi Trinity, I would think the key here is 'inflammation'. Adrenal fatigue is a stress response to inflammation - I'm thinking that there is something he is consuming in his foods that is causing excessive inflammation............... you do not mention anything about foods he has been consuming - is he following Epipaleo, primal or the like? Has he eliminated wheat and perhaps even dairy? Is he consuming plenty of saturated fats and protein?........

### gsteppe14@gmail.com — 2013-08-22T07:39:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-my-results-back-and-it-is-enough-to-make-my-head-spin-help-please.7654/post-93394>

Are you sure it's only 1000mg of fish oil? Twice a day, it's still only barely above a teaspoon. But I can understand if you don't wanna overdo fish oil. Your Armour isn't quite replenishing your FT3. How are your symptoms? Feeling good? So so? I used to take Align. Doing anything for you? What in particular are your digestive or gut flora issues?

Your numbers look good in many areas. I can see that you have no hint of autoimmunity. So many times, whenever I see blood sugar issues or autoimmnity, I can tell it right away. You may have other issues but have none in those areas. Excellent blood sugar numbers overall and lipids.

One issue I have is your liver enzymes. Why are they elevated. Your Albumin also isn't quite as high as it should be. Are you recovering from fatty liver? Had past liver issues? That's the only metabolic issue I see but it's a minor one, especially if you had past issues.

You're supplementing iron because of low ferritin or anemia? I don't see anemia and your low-normal ferritin (and it isn't really that bad; it could possibly be optimal) could be due to your hypo.

Aside from that, I'd get your B12 up above 1000. 1000-1700 is the optimal level. The reference range is set by those who're deficient in B12.

# Thread: Got Some More Labs - Frustrated With My Doctor
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-some-more-labs-frustrated-with-my-doctor.7655/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs

### fireforlunch — 2013-07-17T22:32:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-some-more-labs-frustrated-with-my-doctor.7655/post-90289>

Went to a new Dr. last week. She agreed to all the tests I requested but the ASI.

She said she didn't think it was necessary.

I refuted but she wouldn't budge.

Here are the results:

Bold are my results with normals to the right

Sex Hormone Bind Globulin **34 nmol/L** 10-57

Pregnenolone **72 ng/dL** 33-248

Testosterone, Free **10 ng/dL** 9-30

Testosterone, Total **336 ng/dL** 240-950

Thyroid Peroxidase Ab **13 U/mL** <=34

DHEA Sulfate **282 ug/dL** 120-520

T4 (THYROXINE) TOTAL **5.6 ug/dL** 4.9-11.7
T3 UPTAKE **32.2 %** 22.0-35.0
TSH **3.96 uIU/mL** 0.35-4.94

PSA, TOTAL **0.33 ng/mL** <4.00

ESTRADIOL **<10 pg/mL** 11-44

My TSH came down from 4.89 to 3.96 and even though I still feel like crap she says it's within normal range.

She even says the Testosterone numbers are normal even though they are on the low end.

She wasn't concerned about my Estradiol even though it's less than 10.

Since everything is "normal" in her mind she won't give me anything but offered to refer me to an Endo (for thyroid) and a Urologist for the Testosterone.

I'm so discouraged.

The numbers are clearly out of wack yet she says there is no need for action.

Considering the numbers above what Dr would you recommending seeing first - Endo or Urologist?

Thanks for your input

### Dextery — 2013-07-17T23:53:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-some-more-labs-frustrated-with-my-doctor.7655/post-90295>

You need to find another doctor that is at the very least a low carb, high fat doctor. There are so many people out there that say the same thing..."the doc says my thyroid numbers are within range, but I still feel like crap."

This is a database of paleo type doctors to search for one in your area.
[http://paleophysiciansnetwork.com/](http://paleophysiciansnetwork.com/)
[http://lowcarbdoctors.blogspot.com/](http://lowcarbdoctors.blogspot.com/)
[http://primaldocs.com/](http://primaldocs.com/)

I would also encourage you to read the iodine thread. Iodine plays a huge role in proper thyroid function.

Iodine video that has been posted before: It is estimated that 90-95% of the population is Iodine deficient. And the chlorine, bromine & fluorine has replaced what iodine you did have in the thyroid gland....thus not functioning well. Grizz is the Iodine expert on Dr. Brownstein's protocol for thyroid.

\[video=youtube;uc4Q3kzBSc4\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player\_embedded&v=uc4Q3kzBSc4\[/video\]

And this 5 part video by Dr Tennant. "An Understanding of How the Body Works" After this one plays, find Part 2 2/5, then Part 2 3/5 on out to Part 2 5/5.
2/5 starts him talking about Iodine
[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBbYU7Tp0co](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QBbYU7Tp0co)

If your thyroid ain't functioning well, you will feel like crap.

**Consider someone who have a prescription pad and will use it when you ask**.
That is per-requisite #1.
That is the hardest part.
All other questions come latter.

Forget MD or 99.99% of them

Try your luck with osteopaths and other specialties when able to write scripts.
That varies from state to state.

There is lots of medical advisers (who can't write scripts) engaged in hormonal manipulations.
They work by feel, guess, magic wand and saliva tests.
I would stay away from those.

But you Mom or Grandma may have doc that she knows for the last 45 years, he may be willing to bail you out.
///////

You already know lots of bad stuff about you.
Some of it is easy to correct using otc pills.

I would suggest that you do not exercise or do strenuous work until you come with better numbers.

//////

You may find this post interesting and couple further down.

[http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508)

\\

//

Next time instead of ASI ask for

Cortisol AM/PM
Cortisol (7:30AM)(noon)(2:30PM)

Saliva is to exotic for most, but LabCorp have it.

\=============================
LabCorp
Salivary Cortisol, MS
Test Number: 500179 CPT Code: 82533
eight (8) serial samples
Salivette tubes (item # 51.1532) and
specialized patient collection kits
(item # 38330G)
255.3 Other corticoadrenal overactivity
255.8 Other specified disorders of adrenal glands
255.4 Corticoadrenal insufficiency

\==================================

### MsYo — 2013-07-18T21:00:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-some-more-labs-frustrated-with-my-doctor.7655/post-90341>

Consider finding an anti-aging doctor for your requests. I was fortunate to find a physician that specializes in weightloss and anti-aging (before I found this forum). I started slowly with detailed discussions on how I felt. He was the first to stress the importance of sleep to me when all other doctors did not. When I ask for labs he doesn't resist. I believe he has learned that I am an educated patient and my requests can be substantiated. Not all doctors work this way but keep searching to find one that will work with you. Also, you could pay for the ASI out of pocket. I prepay for the test even though I have insurance.

I have low morning cortisol too. I just followed JanZ suggestion on pregnenolone and it has helped me in the last week. I have my scripts for updated ASI, thyroid, spectracell that I will be doing in the next week.

### JanSz — 2013-07-18T22:58:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-some-more-labs-frustrated-with-my-doctor.7655/post-90346>

> [fireforlunch said:](/goto/post?id=90289)
>
> Sex Hormone Bind Globulin **34 nmol/L** 10-57
> Pregnenolone **72 ng/dL** 33-248
> Testosterone, Free **10 ng/dL** 9-30
> Testosterone, Total **336 ng/dL** 240-950

> PSA, TOTAL **0.33 ng/mL** <4.00
> ESTRADIOL **<10 pg/mL** 11-44

> [fireforlunch said:](/goto/post?id=90342)
>
> I have been following the adrenal fatigue RX for the circadian Rhythm issues.
>
> Ovidrel?
>
> I looked that up on Google and the only thing I could find about it is that it helps with women who have infertility issues
>

>
> Are you willing to make dosing recommendations for the Pregnenolone-MLM and DHEA-MLM

It would help to know your
prolactin
LH
FSH
to figure out why your testosterone is low.

If LH and FSH were low
I would want to check your pituitary (pituitary MRI).
I would also ask if you were hit in the head, specially forehead, specially top of nose where eye glasses rest, because that could cause pituitary problems (sometimes).

Think about above, see what you can do.
Pituitary problems may impact other pituitary hormones.

> The Anterior Lobe
>
> Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH)
> FSH
> LH
> Prolactin (PRL)
> Growth Hormone (GH)
> ACTH
> Alpha Melanocyte-Stimulating Hormone (α-MSH)
>
> The Posterior Lobe
> Vasopressin
> Oxytocin

Most docs would use short course of Clomiprene (25-50)mg/day for few months and check if that raises testosterone.
I am not a doctor.
I think that it would be a waste of time (and possibly impair vision).
I suggest
HCG-shot=250iu/EOD
then check TotalTestosterone(TT) within a month.
If TT raised significantly but less than desirable,
increase to 500iu/EOD
see what happens.
If you good with TT, stay on that HCG dose indefinitely
If you are missing on TT,
go back to HCG-shot=250iu/EOD
and
introduce
Testosterone Cypionate or Enanthate 200mg/mL
T-shot=20units/EOD=40mg/EOD=140mg/week average
(With time you will figure out more precise size of test dose, for now that is good ballpark for SHBG=34
actually, (doctor permitting/cooperating) you can go right away to T-shot=30units/EOD

Use this for T & HCG injections.
Inject anywhere you want, (but no eyes, bone, veins, tendons), with one hand only, the other hand is not touching you, preferably put it in your pocket.

> BD Ultra-Fine II U-100 Insulin Syringes Short Needle 31g 3/10cc 5/16in 90/bx Case of 5---$101.27
> [http://www.americandiabeteswholesale.com/product/bd-ultra-fine-insulin-syringes\_3329\_143.htm](http://www.americandiabeteswholesale.com/product/bd-ultra-fine-insulin-syringes_3329_143.htm)

You really are looking for a testosterone dose that would push down your SHBG to =20
but not lower than 15.

Raising testosterone lowers SHBG
Raising testosterone raise Estradiol
Raising Estrogens raise SHBG
Balancing act.
You need to raise TT and estrogens.

At this time, (with currently low E2 levels), make sure that you are not using any Aromatase inhibitors or promoters to metabolize estrogens.
Anastrozole=Arimidex, I3C, DIM, cruciferous vegetables (cabbage, Brussels sprouts, cauliflower are out at this time, that may change rapidly)

You must monitor hemoglobin Hgb level.
Raising testosterone (in some people) raises Hgb excessively.

Hgb=15 ok, but you do not want less (eat lots of liver)
Hgb=16 ideal
Hgb=17 still ok but you do not want more than that

### Martin — 2013-07-19T16:16:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-some-more-labs-frustrated-with-my-doctor.7655/post-90364>

Interesting subject, those pituitary labs. I looked into them not long ago and here they are for review/opinion/comparison:

DHEA-S **154.0** ug/dl 38-313

Testosterone, Free **5.5** 7.2- 24.0

ACTH **43** 7.2-63.3pg/ml

renin and aldosterone .**82**ng/ml/hr

Aldosterone **2.4** ng/dl 0-30

DHT **30** 30-85

GNRH **<10** pg/ml <25

Prolactin **3.6** ng/ml 4-15.2

IGF-1 **142** ng/ml 81-225

aMSH **<8** 35-81

TSH **2.68** uIU/ml

T4 **7.7** ug/dl 4.5-12

FT4 **2.3** 1.2-4.9

T3 **92** ng/dl 71-180

Estradiol **20.6** pg/ml 1-47

As you can see, many are in the crapper. Standouts for me are: a-MSH below 8, IGF-1-low, Free T-dismal, DHEA-S-sad, Prolactin-ugh, etc. Doc says there are still some hormones in there working, but overall a andropause sorta feel to the whole thing. NO fun, lemme tell ya.

Just had these labs drawn:
Total T **645** 152-669 ng/dl
T, Free **7.94** 3.2-12.2 ng/dl
T, Bioavailable **165** 76-289 ng/dl
T, % Free **1.2** 1.4-2.9
SHGB **77** 12-59 nmol/L

JanZ, my astute forum-mate, what's your take on these?

# Thread: The search for DHEA led me to procaine...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-search-for-dhea-led-me-to-procaine.7661/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2013-07-18T19:53:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-search-for-dhea-led-me-to-procaine.7661/post-90339>

To make a long story short DHEA as you know it is almost impossible to come by here. However we do have it under the name prasterone. So far so good. However,it comes in a mix with procaine and B12 . Reading up on Procaine it seems it is the normal anesthetic and in a derivative a miracle "drug" from the sixties that was subsequently banned by the FDA for reasons of it being unapproved .
Limited studies show it to be more than interesting.
I am torn as to whether I will try it or not.
Composition of each tablet : cianocobalamina (VIT B12) 5.000mcg, sulfato sÃ³dico de prasterona (DHEA sodium sulphate) 20mg, clorhidrato de procaÃ­na/ procaine chlorhydrate 50mg,

Has anyone read more on it , tried it if they are not in the US or generally have an opinion?
Here are some wikis that do a good sum up of what the stuff is about.

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Procaine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Procaine)
[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Procaine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Procaine)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-remove-fluoride-naturally.7664/post-90404>

Holy Basil is easy to get. I found a link about using the crushed Moringa seeds to remove up to 90% of fluoride from water, however Moringa is not readily available everywhere. Looks like polyelectrolytes come to the rescue again.
[http://www.pharmaresearchlibrary.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/05/PRL2013-IJCPS-17031.pdf](http://www.pharmaresearchlibrary.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/05/PRL2013-IJCPS-17031.pdf)

# Thread: "Cold air " thermogenesis ?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2013-07-19T21:20:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/post-90392>

We get very few really cold days here . For the next nine days we have a fantastic polar front coming in from the Antarctic with maybe just a very unlikely maybe of some snowflurries. So Ia m planning to make use of it to the max if it is worthwhile.
Do walks underdressed in thin clothes in the cold count for thermogenesis and reap the benefits?
Is exercising a good idea in the cold?
Any other ideas you have greatly appreciated. Thank you.

### Hemming — 2013-07-19T21:30:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/post-90393>

To just activate BAT there seems to be quite a lot of evidence that cold air is enough. Check out this link [https://www.coolfatburner.com/the-Science.htm#Is\_any\_of\_this\_backed\_up\_by\_science](https://www.coolfatburner.com/the-Science.htm#Is_any_of_this_backed_up_by_science) .

### Lahelada — 2013-07-19T21:41:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/post-90394>

I like ! Thank you Hemming for posting the link. Of all the CT sites I have seen this is not one of them.

### Dextery — 2013-07-19T21:43:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/post-90395>

Janz who lives in New Jersey said he was out strolling in the cold with only shorts and a light T shirt when he was stopped and questioned by a policeman as to his "odd" clothing in 35 F weather. LOL

### Hemming — 2013-07-19T22:43:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/post-90399>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=90394)
>
> I like ! Thank you Hemming for posting the link. Of all the CT sites I have seen this is not one of them.

No problem. You can wear a cap, gloves and warm socks while keeping the rest of the body more exposed to better handle the cold weather but still get a big exposure. In my own experience, and from what I've heard from others too, it's easy to deal with cold if your feet and hands are warm - I personally never wear a cap in the winter.

Maybe someone can comment on if any particular foods/supplements (in addition to bitter lemon extract) can increase the activation and recruitment of BAT.

### nonchalant — 2013-07-19T23:00:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/post-90403>

> [Dextery said:](/goto/post?id=90395)
>
> Janz who lives in New Jersey said he was out strolling in the cold with only shorts and a light T shirt when he was stopped and questioned by a policeman as to his "odd" clothing in 35 F weather. LOL

Sometimes you can find some late summer clothing that looks warm at first glance, but it is quite airy. Gauze-type fabric. I'll wear that during the winter. People have to be fairly close to see that my long-sleeved shirt (that I am wearing over a tank-top) is quite see-through and cool. People stare less if you have shorts on with a long-sleeved top.

### MeghanK — 2013-07-20T18:43:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/post-90444>

@ Hemming.....Bitter Lemon Extract and BAT activation?

### Hemming — 2013-07-20T18:49:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/post-90445>

> [MeghanK said:](/goto/post?id=90444)
>
> @ Hemming.....Bitter Lemon Extract and BAT activation?

Yes, I can't give you the scientific explanation but Jack has mentioned it in the CT FAQ and a quick google search showed some studies too.

### MeghanK — 2013-07-20T18:55:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-air-thermogenesis.7665/post-90446>

Is it a supplement you buy and take?

### Hemming — 2013-07-20T19:13:46+0300
